proposals.title: Structural and Functional Performance of Coconut Meat Protein Hydrolysates in Composite Starch-Protein Gels for Dysphagia Management
proposals.motivation: The demand for nutritious, texture-modified foods for dysphagia patients is growing. While plant proteins like flaxseed are used, their bioactivity and flavor can be limiting. Coconut meat protein hydrolysates (CMPHs) are promising pre-2026 due to their reported antioxidant and antihypertensive bioactivities, but their stability and functional performance in complex food matrices like composite gels are unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating CMPHs into a mung bean starch-flaxseed protein composite gel matrix will enhance its bioactive properties (e.g., antioxidant capacity) without compromising the critical textural, rheological, and water-immobilizing properties required for safe dysphagia food.
proposals.method: CMPHs will be produced via enzymatic hydrolysis of defatted coconut flour. They will be incorporated at varying levels (0.5-2.0% w/w) into a composite gel system based on the researcher's prior work (mung bean starch and flaxseed protein). The method will systematically assess: 1) Gel network formation via rheology (small-amplitude oscillatory shear) and texture profile analysis, 2) Water-holding capacity and syneresis, 3) In-vitro antioxidant activity (DPPH, ABTS assays) of the final gel, and 4) Accelerated stability testing of bioactivity during chilled storage.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first practical formulation protocol for integrating bioactive CMPHs into a viscoelastic food matrix for special diets. It will establish critical formulation boundaries for maintaining texture-safety while delivering functionality, directly advancing the field of bioactive, plant-based dysphagia foods.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond characterizing CMPHs in isolation or simple model systems, instead testing their performance in a complex, clinically relevant composite gel matrix where protein-polysaccharide interactions dictate safety and quality, a key gap pre-2026.

proposals.title: Comparative Analysis of Plant Protein Hydrolysates: Gelation, Emulsifying, and Water-Immobilizing Properties Beyond Coconut Meat
proposals.motivation: Plant protein hydrolysates are promising functional ingredients for structuring foods, including dysphagia diets. While coconut meat hydrolysate has shown specific benefits, it is unclear if its effects on protein gel networks are unique or shared by hydrolysates from other major plant proteins. A systematic comparison is needed to guide the rational selection of protein sources for tailored food textures and to understand structure-function relationships in composite protein-polysaccharide systems, a key pre-2026 research gap.
proposals.hypothesis: The functional improvements in gel properties (e.g., water immobilization, texture) induced by coconut protein hydrolysate are not unique but will vary systematically based on the source plant protein's inherent composition and the degree of hydrolysis, with legume and cereal hydrolysates showing comparable or superior effects.
proposals.method: We will produce hydrolysates from flaxseed, mung bean, and pea proteins using Alcalase, controlling for degree of hydrolysis (DH 5%, 10%, 15%). These will be incorporated into composite gel models with mung bean starch, mirroring our previous work. Gel properties will be characterized via small-deformation rheology (temperature sweeps), large-deformation texture analysis (TPA), and water-holding capacity. The emulsifying activity index (EAI) of each hydrolysate will be measured to correlate with gel network formation. Myofibrillar protein gel models will also be tested to assess cross-application.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a comparative framework for plant protein hydrolysate functionality in gel matrices, directly informing ingredient selection for specialized foods. It will elucidate whether benefits are source-specific or generalizable, advancing fundamental knowledge of how hydrolysis modifies protein-polysaccharide and protein-protein interactions in soft solid networks.
proposals.novelty: While individual plant protein functionalities are studied, a direct, controlled comparison of hydrolysates from diverse sources within identical composite gel systems is lacking. This moves beyond characterizing single ingredients to establishing predictive relationships between protein source, hydrolysis, and multi-scale gel properties.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Muscle Protein Hydrolysate Gels with Targeted Antioxidant Activity Post-Simulated Gastrointestinal Digestion
proposals.motivation: Bioactive protein hydrolysates are promising functional ingredients, but their final bioactivity is unpredictable due to complex digestion-driven modifications. Current approaches are trial-and-error, starting with a hydrolysate and measuring its digested activity. An inverse design framework would enable the rational creation of protein-based foods, like gels for dysphagia or muscle foods, with precise, tailored health functionalities, addressing a major gap in precision nutrition and functional food development.
proposals.hypothesis: By employing an inverse problem-solving framework, it is possible to identify the initial protein source, hydrolysis parameters, and composite gel network structure (e.g., with starch or polysaccharides) that will yield a pre-defined antioxidant peptide profile and activity after simulated gastrointestinal digestion.
proposals.method: We will first generate a diverse library of porcine myofibrillar protein hydrolysates via systematic enzymatic hydrolysis (e.g., using Alcalase, Flavourzyme). Their composite gels will be formed with mung bean starch and Astragalus polysaccharide, informed by your prior work. A forward model will be built using machine learning (e.g., Random Forest or a simple multilayer perceptron) to map initial hydrolysate properties (e.g., degree of hydrolysis, peptide size distribution) and gel network parameters (from rheology/texture) to post-digestion antioxidant profiles (ORAC, ABTS). An inverse design algorithm, such as a Bayesian optimization loop, will then iteratively query this model to recommend initial conditions that minimize the difference between predicted and a predefined target antioxidant activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, data-driven methodology for the targeted design of bioactive protein gels, moving the field from descriptive analysis to predictive engineering. It will provide specific formulation rules for creating muscle protein-starch composite gels with guaranteed antioxidant functionality, directly applicable to functional and medical foods. The generated dataset and model will be a foundational resource for inverse design in food protein science.
proposals.novelty: While forward analysis of protein digestibility is common, applying an inverse problem framework from engineering to design a food protein system for a specific digested bioactivity is a paradigm shift. It uniquely integrates your expertise in protein gels, composites, and muscle protein activity into a predictive computational design strategy.

proposals.title: Systematic Comparison of Gelation and Emulsifying Properties of Enzymatically Hydrolyzed Legume and Cereal Protein Isolates
proposals.motivation: Plant proteins are crucial for sustainable food systems, but their functional properties vary widely, limiting their application as direct replacements for animal proteins in structured foods like gels. A standardized, comparative study of how controlled enzymatic hydrolysis modifies the gelation and emulsifying properties of diverse plant protein sources is lacking but essential for rational ingredient selection and optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the impact of enzymatic hydrolysis on gel strength, water-holding capacity, and emulsifying activity will be highly dependent on the protein source (e.g., mung bean, pea, soy, rice, oat), due to inherent differences in protein composition, structure, and aggregation behavior.
proposals.method: We will isolate proteins from mung bean, pea, soy, rice, and oat. A standardized protocol using Alcalase at controlled hydrolysis times (0, 15, 30, 60 min) will be applied to all isolates. Gel properties will be characterized using small-deformation rheology (gel strength, storage modulus G'), large-deformation texture analysis (hardness, springiness), and water-holding capacity. Emulsifying activity and stability will be measured using the turbidimetric method. Statistical analysis (ANOVA, PCA) will correlate hydrolysis degree with functional metrics across sources.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, quantitative comparison of the functional potential of major plant proteins post-hydrolysis, generating a dataset to guide formulators. It will elucidate structure-function relationships between protein source, hydrolysis extent, and gel/emulsion performance, advancing the fundamental understanding of plant protein modification.
proposals.novelty: While hydrolysis studies exist for individual proteins, a systematic, side-by-side comparison using a consistent hydrolysis and testing protocol across a spectrum of legumes and cereals is novel. This approach directly isolates the effect of protein source from methodological variability.

proposals.title: Enhancing the Stability and Controlled Release of Bioactive Peptides in Composite Gels via Starch-Protein Coacervate Microencapsulation
proposals.motivation: Bioactive peptides derived from muscle or plant proteins offer significant health benefits but are often unstable during food processing and digestion, limiting their efficacy. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Xiong's work on protein-starch composite gels and emulsifying properties, highlights the need for delivery systems that protect these sensitive compounds while integrating them into complex food matrices like gels for dysphagia or functional foods.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that microencapsulating bioactive peptide fractions within a composite shell of mung bean starch and flaxseed protein via complex coacervation will significantly enhance their stability against environmental stressors and enable controlled, pH-triggered release within simulated gastrointestinal conditions.
proposals.method: Active peptide fractions will be isolated from porcine myofibrillar protein hydrolysates. A complex coacervation method will be optimized to form microcapsules using mung bean starch and flaxseed protein, building on Dr. Xiong's prior composite gel work. The encapsulation efficiency, morphology (via SEM), and in-vitro release profile under simulated gastric/intestinal pH will be characterized. The stabilized peptides will then be incorporated into a model composite gel system, and the gel's rheological properties, water-holding capacity, and peptide retention after processing will be evaluated.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, food-grade encapsulation strategy specifically designed for peptide stabilization within viscoelastic gel networks. It will deliver practical formulation guidelines for incorporating bioactive peptides into texture-modified foods, directly contributing to the development of next-generation functional and medical nutrition products with enhanced bioactivity.
proposals.novelty: While microencapsulation is known, its application specifically within the context of protein-starch composite gel matrices for dysphagia, using coacervates derived from the same biopolymers that form the gel network itself, is a novel and synergistic approach not extensively explored pre-2026.

proposals.title: Predicting Plant Protein Gelation via Polymer Physics: A Coarse-Grained Model for Flaxseed and Pea Protein Aggregation Networks
proposals.motivation: Plant proteins are crucial for sustainable food systems, but their inconsistent gelation behavior compared to animal proteins hinders their use in structured foods like meat analogs and dysphagia diets. Current optimization relies heavily on empirical trial-and-error, lacking predictive frameworks to link protein molecular properties to final gel network structure and rheology. A physics-based model could accelerate the rational design of plant protein ingredients.
proposals.hypothesis: The aggregation and gelation kinetics of plant proteins can be quantitatively predicted by modeling them as semi-flexible charged polymers, where gel elasticity and water-holding capacity are determined by the interplay of hydrophobic attraction, electrostatic repulsion, and cross-link density.
proposals.method: We will develop a coarse-grained molecular dynamics (CGMD) model using the LAMMPS software, representing flaxseed and pea protein isolates as bead-spring chains with tunable hydrophobicity and charge. The model will incorporate established potentials for van der Waals, screened electrostatic (Debye-Hückel), and explicit bond-angle stiffness. Aggregation kinetics under thermal treatment will be simulated and validated against experimental rheology (small-amplitude oscillatory shear), texture profile analysis, and water immobilization data from composite gels, building on the researcher's prior work with starch-protein systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles computational tool to predict plant protein gel properties from sequence and solvent conditions, moving the field beyond phenomenological correlations. It will establish design rules for modifying proteins or processing conditions to achieve target gel strengths and microstructures, directly informing the development of novel food textures.
proposals.novelty: While polymer physics models have been applied to synthetic polymers and amyloid fibrils, their systematic application to predict the functional aggregation of complex, polydisperse food plant proteins is novel. The integration of this theoretical framework with the researcher's experimental expertise in composite gels and muscle protein analogs creates a unique cross-disciplinary approach.

proposals.title: Integrating Mung Bean and Flaxseed Supply Chains: A Techno-Functional and Economic Analysis for Scaling Novel Protein-Starch Composite Gels
proposals.motivation: The global demand for sustainable, non-allergenic, and functionally diverse alternative proteins is growing, yet supply chains remain heavily reliant on soy and pea. Scaling novel sources like mung bean and flaxseed is hindered by a lack of integrated data linking agricultural yield, protein extraction efficiency, and the critical functional properties (e.g., gelation, water immobilization) required for high-value food applications like dysphagia diets. Pre-2026 research has shown the promise of these proteins but not their scalable production pathway.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by modeling the relationship between regional cultivation practices, protein extraction protocols, and the resulting gelation properties of mung bean starch-flaxseed protein composites, we can identify an optimized, economically viable supply chain configuration that meets specific functional benchmarks for food texture.
proposals.method: The research will employ a multi-stage approach. First, we will cultivate mung bean and flaxseed under varied agronomic conditions (irrigation, fertilization) to create raw material batches with defined protein content and quality. Second, using methods from the researcher's prior work, we will process these batches into protein concentrates and starches and fabricate composite gels at defined ratios. Third, we will rigorously characterize the gels using rheology, texture profile analysis (TPA), and water-holding capacity to establish property databases. Finally, we will apply techno-economic analysis (TEA) modeling to link agricultural inputs, processing yields, and functional performance to total cost, identifying optimal supply chain nodes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-of-its-kind, quantitative framework for scaling novel protein crops based on their techno-functional performance, not just crude protein yield. It will deliver a validated model for predicting gel properties from upstream supply chain variables and a comparative economic analysis of mung bean and flaxseed against established proteins for specific food applications.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of agricultural supply chain variables with detailed protein functionality analysis (gel networks) and TEA, moving beyond siloed studies of either agronomy or food chemistry to address the holistic scaling challenge for underutilized protein sources.

proposals.title: Thermal Pre-treatment of Flaxseed Protein Isolate: Impact on Enzymatic Hydrolysis Efficiency and Antioxidant Activity of Resultant Peptides
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research demonstrates that controlled enzymatic hydrolysis of plant proteins, like flaxseed protein, is a key strategy for generating bioactive peptides with antioxidant properties for functional foods. However, standard processing often involves a heating step for protein isolation or pasteurization, which can irreversibly alter protein structure. The effect of this common thermal pre-treatment on the subsequent enzymatic release and activity of peptides is not fully understood, posing a potential bottleneck for optimizing bioactive ingredient production.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that preheating flaxseed protein isolate will induce structural aggregation and reduce enzyme accessibility, thereby decreasing the efficiency of hydrolysis and the in vitro antioxidant activity (e.g., DPPH radical scavenging, ABTS assay) of the resulting peptide fractions compared to hydrolysis of a non-preheated control.
proposals.method: Flaxseed protein isolate (FPI) will be prepared and split. One portion will undergo a controlled thermal treatment (e.g., 80°C for 30 min, simulating common pasteurization). Both native and preheated FPI will be subjected to hydrolysis under identical conditions using proteases relevant to prior work (e.g., Alcalase, Flavourzyme) and a gastrointestinal enzyme simulation (pepsin followed by pancreatin). Hydrolysis degree will be monitored via pH-stat and OPA assay. Peptide fractions (<3 kDa) will be collected via ultrafiltration. Antioxidant activity will be quantified using DPPH, ABTS, and ferric reducing antioxidant power (FRAP) assays. Gel properties (if any) of the hydrolysates will be assessed via small-deformation rheology.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly quantify the trade-off between thermal processing (for safety/functionality) and bioactivity generation in plant protein systems. It will provide practical guidance for designing hydrolysis protocols to maximize peptide yield and activity. The findings will extend Dr. Xiong's expertise in protein composite gels to the realm of bioactive peptide production from underutilized plant proteins.
proposals.novelty: While heat's effect on myofibrillar protein gelation is well-studied in Dr. Xiong's prior work, its specific impact on the *enzymatic releasability* and activity of antioxidant peptides from plant proteins is less explored. The novelty lies in systematically linking a common industrial pre-treatment to downstream biochemical accessibility and biofunctionality, rather than just gel texture.

proposals.title: Elucidating the pH-Driven Disulfide Cleavage and Subunit Dissociation Mechanism of Soybean Glycinin (11S) Using Spectroscopic and Gelation Analysis
proposals.motivation: Soybean glycinin (11S) is a major plant protein whose functional properties, such as gelation, are critically modulated by pH-induced structural changes. The dissociation of its hexameric structure via disulfide bond cleavage is a key step determining its gel network formation, but the precise molecular mechanism and sequence of events during pH-shifting remain poorly characterized. A detailed understanding is essential for rationally designing plant-based foods with tailored textures and for improving protein functionality in composite gels, a key area of dysphagia food development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dissociation of the glycinin hexamer during pH-shifting is initiated by the protonation of specific acidic residues, which induces a conformational strain leading to the selective cleavage of inter-subunit disulfide bonds, and that the rate and extent of this dissociation directly dictate the resulting gel's rheological and water-immobilizing properties.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-technique approach: (1) Controlled pH-shift treatments (e.g., from pH 7.5 to 3.0) of purified glycinin, monitoring dissociation kinetics via size-exclusion chromatography and non-reducing SDS-PAGE. (2) Molecular-level probing using intrinsic fluorescence spectroscopy and synchronous fluorescence to track tryptophan microenvironment changes, and Raman spectroscopy to monitor disulfide bond stretching vibrations (S-S at ~510 cm⁻¹) and tyrosine doublet ratio. (3) Correlating dissociation states with functional outcomes by measuring the rheological properties (storage/loss moduli via dynamic oscillatory rheometry) and water-holding capacity of gels formed at different stages of the dissociation process.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic map linking specific pH-induced molecular events (disulfide cleavage, subunit separation) to the macroscopic gelation properties of glycinin. It will contribute fundamental knowledge to the field of plant protein physics and offer a scientific basis for optimizing processing conditions (like pH-shifting) to control protein network structure in gels, particularly for applications in textured foods and specialized nutrition.
proposals.novelty: While pH effects on 11S are known, this study uniquely integrates real-time spectroscopic tracking of disulfide status and protein conformation with precise gelation analysis to establish causal, sequential links in the dissociation mechanism, moving beyond descriptive observations to a predictive model.

proposals.title: Synergistic effects of high-pressure homogenization and pH-shifting on the structural modification and gelation properties of plant proteins
proposals.motivation: The pH-shifting method is a well-established technique for modifying protein functionality, particularly for improving solubility and emulsifying properties by inducing controlled unfolding and refolding. However, its ability to precisely control subsequent aggregation and network formation for gelation is limited. High-pressure homogenization (HPH) is another physical modification known to alter protein structure and improve texture. Combining these two non-thermal techniques could offer unprecedented control over protein conformational states, addressing a key challenge in creating plant-based or composite gels with tailored rheological and water-holding properties for food applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying high-pressure homogenization concurrently with or immediately after the pH-shifting process will synergistically enhance protein unfolding, disrupt non-covalent aggregates more effectively, and lead to the formation of a more homogeneous and finer protein network upon gelation, resulting in superior gel strength, elasticity, and water immobilization compared to either treatment alone.
proposals.method: The research will use flaxseed protein isolate (FPI) and mung bean protein isolate (MBPI) as model plant proteins, given the researcher's experience. Proteins will be subjected to a standard pH-shifting treatment (e.g., to pH 12, held, then neutralized). The critical innovation is integrating HPH (at pressures of 50-150 MPa) at specific stages: during the alkaline unfolding phase and/or immediately after neutralization. The structural changes will be characterized using circular dichroism, surface hydrophobicity, and particle size analysis. Gelation properties will be evaluated by preparing composite gels with mung bean starch, measuring rheological parameters (storage/loss moduli), texture profile analysis (hardness, springiness), and water-holding capacity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel combined physical-chemical modification protocol for plant proteins. It will provide fundamental insights into the interplay of shear forces and electrostatic repulsion/attraction in directing protein aggregation pathways. The expected outcome is a method to engineer protein gels with precisely modulated textures, directly contributing to the development of next-generation structured plant-based foods or specialized nutritional products like dysphagia diets.
proposals.novelty: While both pH-shifting and HPH have been studied independently for protein modification, their sequential or integrated application to deliberately steer the unfolding-aggregation-gelation continuum is novel. This approach moves beyond using HPH merely for particle size reduction, positioning it as a dynamic tool to interact with transient protein states created by pH-shifting.

proposals.title: Structural and Functional Consequences of High-Intensity Emulsification on pH-Shifted Plant Proteins: A Pathway to Tailored Composite Gels
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on pH-shifting has established it as a powerful method to modify protein solubility and functionality, particularly for plant proteins. However, these modified proteins are often subsequently used as emulsifiers in complex food matrices, and the intense mechanical and interfacial forces during emulsification may further alter their structure in ways that are not understood. This knowledge gap limits the rational design of protein-based gels and composites, such as those for dysphagia foods or meat analogs, where emulsification is a critical processing step.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-intensity emulsification induces distinct, quantifiable structural changes in pH-shifted proteins (unfolding, aggregation state, surface hydrophobicity) that are non-identical to those caused by pH-shifting alone, and that these changes dictate the subsequent gelation properties and water-immobilizing capacity of the protein or protein-starch composite.
proposals.method: Flaxseed and mung bean protein will be subjected to pH-shift treatment (alkaline then neutralization). The treated proteins will then undergo high-pressure homogenization to create oil-in-water emulsions at defined energy inputs. Structural changes will be characterized using spectroscopy (circular dichroism, fluorescence for surface hydrophobicity), particle size analysis, and electrophoresis. The emulsified proteins will then be used to form pure protein gels and composite gels with mung bean starch, with their rheological (small and large deformation), textural, and water-holding properties rigorously measured and correlated to the structural metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a cause-effect map linking emulsification processing parameters to specific protein structural states and final gel functionalities. It will provide a foundational principle for designing composite food gels with targeted textures, directly benefiting the development of next-generation tailored foods for specific nutritional needs and consumer groups. The findings will advance the field from empirical formulation to a more predictive, physics-based approach.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Xiong and others has studied how protein modification affects emulsification, this proposal inverts the question to systematically investigate how emulsification itself acts as a secondary, forceful modifier of already-unfolded proteins, a sequential processing effect not previously isolated and detailed in the context of gel network formation.

proposals.title: Emulsion Stability as a High-Throughput Probe for Heat-Induced Conformational States of Myofibrillar Proteins in Composite Gels
proposals.motivation: Controlling protein gelation is critical for food texture, especially in muscle protein and composite gel systems. Current methods for characterizing protein conformational changes (e.g., spectroscopy, calorimetry) are low-throughput and not easily applied to complex matrices. A rapid, indirect method to screen protein structural states would accelerate the formulation of functional foods and dysphagia diets.
proposals.hypothesis: The emulsifying activity index (EAI) and emulsion stability index (ESI) of myofibrillar proteins, measured before gelation, will correlate strongly with specific, heat-induced conformational states and predict the final rheological and water-binding properties of composite protein-starch gels.
proposals.method: We will extract porcine myofibrillar protein (MPI) and subject it to controlled thermal treatments (40-80°C) to induce distinct unfolding/aggregation states. For each state, we will immediately measure EAI and ESI using a standard oil-water emulsification protocol with turbidimetric analysis. These MPI samples will then be used to form composite gels with mung bean starch, and the gels will be characterized for rheology (small-amplitude oscillatory shear), texture profile analysis, and water-holding capacity. Correlation analysis (e.g., Pearson coefficients, principal component regression) will link emulsion stability metrics to gel properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, high-throughput screening tool for predicting gel functionality from simple emulsification tests. It will provide new mechanistic insight into how protein conformational transitions, probed via interfacial behavior, dictate network formation in composite matrices, directly benefiting the rational design of tailored-texture foods.
proposals.novelty: While emulsion properties are often studied as functional endpoints, using them as a diagnostic proxy for specific protein conformational states in a gelation context is a novel, dual-purpose approach. This inverts the standard paradigm, treating emulsification data as an analytical input rather than just a functional output.

proposals.title: Engineering and Characterization of a Novel Lipid-Continuous Composite: Protein Gel Particles as a Dispersed Phase for Tailored Fat Replacement and Textural Design
proposals.motivation: Traditional meat and dairy analogues often use protein gels as a continuous matrix with fat as a filler, limiting fat reduction and texture control. Inverting this paradigm by dispersing pre-formed protein gel particles within a continuous lipid phase could create novel composites with unique rheological and sensory properties. This approach, leveraging pre-2026 knowledge of protein-polysaccharide gelation and emulsification, offers a direct route to engineer reduced-fat, high-protein, or calorically-dense structured foods for specific nutritional needs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by controlling the size, rigidity, and volume fraction of pre-formed myofibrillar or plant protein gel particles, we can dictate the macroscopic rheology, stability, and water-immobilizing capacity of a lipid-continuous composite, enabling the design of materials with textures ranging from spreadable to self-standing.
proposals.method: We will prepare porcine myofibrillar protein and flaxseed protein isolate gels, modulating their rigidity with eugenol and Astragalus polysaccharide based on prior work. These gels will be comminuted into calibrated micro-particles. Using high-shear emulsification, these particles will be dispersed as a high-internal-phase filler within continuous lipid matrices (e.g., soybean oil, pork fat). The composite's structure will be characterized via confocal microscopy and rheology, while its stability and textural properties will be assessed using texture profile analysis and centrifugal water/oil holding capacity measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational framework for designing lipid-continuous, protein-rich composites, expanding the toolbox for food material science. It will provide quantitative relationships between protein gel particle properties and composite functionality, directly contributing to the development of next-generation fat-replaced meat products and specialized nutritional foods for conditions like dysphagia or malnutrition.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the canonical meat analogue structure, treating the protein network not as the matrix but as a structured, water-immobilizing filler within fat. It uniquely combines pre-2026 expertise in protein gelation, composite gels, and emulsifying activity to create a system where fat's role is fundamentally redefined from a dispersed filler to a continuous, functional medium.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Mapping of Texture-Stability-Safety Trade-Offs in Sodium-Reduced Myofibrillar Protein-Starch Composite Gels
proposals.motivation: Reducing sodium in processed gelled foods is a critical public health goal, but sodium chloride is essential for protein solubilization, gel network formation, and microbial safety. Current research often optimizes for a single property like gel strength, leading to formulations that fail in real-world applications due to poor water-holding, syneresis, or insufficient shelf-life. A holistic, multi-parameter approach is needed to engineer viable low-sodium solutions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically mapping the functional properties of composite protein-polysaccharide systems under low ionic strength, we can identify non-linear trade-offs and synergistic interactions that enable the design of robust, multi-component gel matrices achieving target texture, stability, and safety simultaneously.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-throughput formulation platform using porcine myofibrillar protein and mung bean starch as a base model, informed by the researcher's prior work. A Design of Experiments (DoE) approach will vary protein:starch ratio, ionic strength (NaCl, KCl, CaCl2 blends), and inclusion of functional polysaccharides (e.g., Astragalus polysaccharide). Each formulation will be screened against a multi-parameter profile: rheology (gel strength, elasticity), texture (TPA), water-immobilizing properties (cooking loss, WHC), and predicted safety via water activity (a_w) measurement and established predictive models (e.g., Norrish equation). Data will be analyzed using multivariate statistics (PCA, PLS regression) to create functionality maps.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive functionality map for sodium-reduced gel systems, identifying critical formulation levers and trade-offs. It will deliver practical, multi-component strategies for significant sodium reduction in meat-based and composite gels. The integrated texture-stability-safety profiling framework will be a novel tool for rational food design.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the simultaneous high-throughput optimization against a holistic functionality profile, moving beyond singular gelation metrics. It integrates pre-2026 knowledge on protein-polysaccharide composites, muscle protein gelation, and water activity prediction into a unified engineering framework specifically for sodium reduction.

proposals.title: Modulating Myofibrillar Protein Gelation at Low Ionic Strength: A Multi-Component Strategy Using Polysaccharides and Plant Proteins
proposals.motivation: High sodium chloride content is essential for solubilizing myofibrillar proteins and forming heat-induced gels in processed meats, but contributes to negative public health outcomes. Current strategies for sodium reduction often lead to unacceptable textural defects and water loss. There is a critical need to develop fundamental, ingredient-based strategies that enable robust protein gelation at significantly lower ionic strengths to facilitate the creation of healthier meat products.
proposals.hypothesis: The targeted incorporation of specific polysaccharides (e.g., Astragalus polysaccharide) and plant proteins (e.g., flaxseed protein) into myofibrillar protein systems can compensate for the reduced electrostatic screening at low ionic strength, promoting protein-protein interactions and water immobilization necessary for forming cohesive, elastic gels.
proposals.method: The research will employ a systematic design, building on Dr. Xiong's expertise. Porcine myofibrillar protein (MP) gels will be formulated at sub-optimal, low ionic strengths (e.g., 0.1-0.3 M NaCl). The effects of incorporating Astragalus polysaccharide (APS) and flaxseed protein isolate (FPI), individually and in combination, will be investigated. Gel properties will be characterized using small-deformation rheology (gelation temperature, storage modulus), large-deformation texture analysis (hardness, springiness), and water-holding capacity measurements. Microstructure will be evaluated using confocal laser scanning microscopy and scanning electron microscopy to correlate network formation with macroscopic properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, multi-component formulation principle for creating low-salt meat protein gels with tailored rheological and textural properties. It will provide mechanistic insights into how polysaccharides and plant proteins modulate protein interactions in low-ionic-strength environments. The findings will directly inform the development of structured, reduced-sodium meat analogues and dysphagia-friendly foods.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has studied polysaccharide-protein composites or salt reduction in isolation, this proposal uniquely integrates these approaches, specifically targeting the fundamental challenge of poor protein solubility and network formation at low ionic strength. It moves beyond simple substitution to actively engineer the gelation pathway using synergistic biocomponents.

proposals.title: Systematic Evaluation of Animal Fat and Plant Oil Diversity on Myofibrillar Protein Gelation and Composite Gel Properties
proposals.motivation: Prior work (e.g., on lard vs. oil) suggests fat type significantly influences protein gel properties, which are critical for food texture, stability, and nutritional delivery. However, it is unclear if these effects are universal or specific to the fat/oil pairs tested. A systematic investigation across a broader spectrum of fats and oils, differing in fatty acid composition, solid fat content, and crystalline structure, is needed to establish generalizable structure-function relationships for designing tailored food gels, including for specialized diets.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed differences in protein gel properties between lard and specific plant oils are not universal but are dictated by the physical state (solid fat content, melting profile) and interfacial composition of the lipid phase, which will vary systematically across different animal fats (e.g., beef tallow, duck fat) and plant oils (e.g., olive, coconut, high-oleic sunflower).
proposals.method: We will prepare composite gels using a standardized porcine myofibrillar protein matrix, incorporating 10% (w/w) of different fats/oils: animal fats (lard, beef tallow, duck fat) and plant oils (soybean, olive, coconut, high-oleic sunflower). Gelation will be induced via a standard heat treatment. We will characterize the lipid phase (fatty acid profile, solid fat content, crystallization behavior) and the composite gels using small-deformation rheology (gel strength, network formation), large-deformation texture analysis (hardness, water-holding capacity), and microscopy (confocal, SEM) to assess network microstructure and water immobilization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a foundational map linking specific lipid physical-chemical properties to functional outcomes in protein-lipid composite gels. It will move the field beyond binary comparisons to predictive understanding, enabling rational selection of fats/oils for target gel textures in processed meats and dysphagia foods. The data will clarify whether 'animal fat vs. plant oil' is a meaningful functional category.
proposals.novelty: While fat substitution studies are common, a controlled, parallel investigation spanning multiple animal fats and plant oils within the same protein gel system to decouple the effects of lipid source, saturation, and physical state is lacking. This directly tests the universality of prior binary comparisons.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Relative Contribution of Disulfide Bonds and Non-Covalent Interactions to the Gelation of Myofibrillar Protein-Starch Composite Systems
proposals.motivation: The formation of robust protein gels, critical for food texture and functionality, relies on a complex interplay of covalent (e.g., disulfide) and non-covalent bonds. However, the precise, quantitative contribution of each bond type to final gel strength and stability remains poorly understood, limiting the rational design of composite gels for applications like dysphagia foods. This knowledge gap hinders the optimization of protein-polysaccharide blends, such as those involving muscle proteins and starches, for specific textural and water-holding properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that disulfide bonds are the primary determinant of the ultimate shear strength and thermal stability of myofibrillar protein gels, while hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions predominantly govern water immobilization and initial network formation during composite gelation with starch.
proposals.method: We will employ a systematic chemical perturbation approach using pre-2026 methods. Porcine myofibrillar protein-mung bean starch composite gels will be treated with specific bond-disrupting agents: N-ethylmaleimide (NEM) for disulfide blockade, urea for hydrogen bonding, and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) for hydrophobic interactions. Gel strength will be quantified via small-deformation rheology (storage modulus G') and large-deformation texture analysis (penetration force). Water mobility will be assessed via low-field NMR T2 relaxometry. The specific contribution of each interaction will be calculated by comparing the property loss in treated gels to controls, using analysis of variance (ANOVA) and principal component analysis (PCA) to deconvolute the effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative breakdown of bond-type contributions in a muscle protein-starch composite gel, moving beyond qualitative descriptions. It will deliver a predictive framework for formulators to select processing conditions or additives that target specific interactions to tailor gel properties. The findings will directly advance the rational design of textured foods, including those for specialized nutritional needs.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies note the importance of various bonds, this proposal uniquely applies a quantitative deconvolution strategy, using specific chemical probes and multi-scale property analysis, to assign precise numerical contributions to gel parameters in a complex, relevant composite system, rather than in isolated protein models.

proposals.title: Deconstructing the Dynamic Assembly Pathway of Myofibrillar Protein-Lipid Composite Gels via In-situ Cryo-Electron Microscopy Under Coupled Shear-pH Processing
proposals.motivation: The creation of structured foods, such as meat analogs or dysphagia diets, relies on controlling protein-lipid gel networks. Current research, including studies on eugenol and composite gels, primarily characterizes final gel properties, leaving the dynamic assembly process during combined physicochemical (pH-shift) and mechanical (shear) treatments as a 'black box'. Understanding this dynamic pathway is critical for rationally designing gels with targeted textures and water-holding capacities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying controlled shear stress during the pH-induced unfolding of myofibrillar proteins directs the assembly of lipid droplets into the protein network, creating distinct, tunable interfacial architectures that dictate the final gel's rheological and water-immobilizing properties.
proposals.method: We will employ a custom shear cell coupled to a rapid pH-stat system to process porcine myofibrillar protein-lipid (e.g., flaxseed oil) mixtures. Samples will be vitrified at precise timepoints during the pH transition under defined shear rates (0-1000 s⁻¹). The nanoscale assembly will be captured using cryo-transmission electron microscopy (cryo-TEM) and cryo-scanning electron microscopy (cryo-SEM). Microstructural metrics (particle size, network porosity, interface thickness) will be quantified from images and directly correlated with simultaneous in-line rheometry and the final gel's textural profile analysis (TPA).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, causal map between dynamic processing parameters and evolving nanoscale structure in protein-lipid composites. It will provide foundational knowledge for designing next-generation food textures, particularly for muscle protein-based applications, by identifying the critical control points in network assembly that determine functional properties like elasticity and water binding.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the *in-situ*, time-resolved correlative microscopy of a coupled shear-pH process, moving beyond endpoint analysis to visualize and quantify how shear flow alters protein unfolding intermediates and directs lipid co-assembly in real-time, a dynamic view not achieved in prior composite gel studies.

proposals.title: Nanostructural Elucidation of Protein-Lipid Interfaces in Composite Gels using Cryo-Scanning Electron Microscopy and Image Analysis
proposals.motivation: The functional properties of composite protein gels, such as those in dysphagia foods or muscle protein analogs, are dictated by their microstructure, particularly the interface between protein networks and embedded lipids or polysaccharides. Pre-2026, conventional microscopy often lacks the resolution to characterize these interfaces in their native, hydrated state, limiting our understanding of how components like eugenol or Astragalus polysaccharide modify gel architecture. A detailed, high-resolution analysis is crucial for rationally designing gels with tailored textural and water-immobilizing properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the incorporation of functional additives (e.g., eugenol, polysaccharides) into protein-starch composite gels induces distinct, quantifiable changes in the nanoscale morphology of the protein-lipid interface and the three-dimensional network connectivity, which directly correlate with macroscopic rheological and textural outcomes.
proposals.method: We will employ cryo-scanning electron microscopy (cryo-SEM) to image freeze-fractured surfaces of mung bean starch-flaxseed protein and porcine myofibrillar protein gel samples, preserving their native hydrated microstructure. Specific image analysis will be conducted using the Fiji/ImageJ platform with algorithms for quantitative network analysis (e.g., skeletonization for pore size distribution, network branching analysis) and interface characterization (e.g, gray-level co-occurrence matrix for texture at the interface). Comparative analysis will be performed on gels formulated with and without key additives identified in prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, nanoscale visualization and quantitative metrics of the protein-lipid interface in these complex composite gels. It will establish clear structure-function relationships, explaining how specific additives modulate gel properties at a fundamental level. The findings will offer a concrete methodological framework for microstructural optimization in food protein gel design.
proposals.novelty: While cryo-SEM has been used in food science, its combined application with advanced image analysis algorithms to quantitatively deconstruct the protein-lipid interface in multi-component gels, directly linking nanoscale features to specific additive effects from the researcher's prior work, is novel and non-obvious.

proposals.title: Interfacial Rheology of Myofibrillar Protein-Starch Composite Layers: A Direct Link to Emulsion Gel Microstructure and Dysphagia Food Texture
proposals.motivation: The textural and water-immobilizing properties of composite protein-starch gels, crucial for applications like dysphagia foods, are governed by the formation and stability of the emulsion network. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Xiong's work, correlates bulk gel properties with ingredients but lacks direct measurement of the fundamental mechanical properties of the proteinaceous layer at the oil-water interface, which dictates droplet stability and network integrity under shear.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the interfacial viscoelastic modulus of the adsorbed protein-starch composite layer, measured directly via interfacial shear and dilatational rheology, is the primary determinant of the bulk rheological strength and fracture properties of the resulting heat-induced emulsion gel.
proposals.method: We will employ a double-wall ring geometry on a rotational rheometer for interfacial shear rheology and a pendant drop tensiometer with oscillatory area change for dilatational rheology. Porcine myofibrillar protein will be combined with mung bean starch at varying ratios, and the evolution of the interfacial storage (G'i) and loss (G''i) moduli at a model oil-water interface will be tracked during adsorption and thermal treatment. These parameters will be correlated with bulk large-deformation rheology and water-holding capacity of the final cooked gels.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative, mechanistic link between directly measured interfacial film mechanics and the macroscopic functional properties of protein-starch emulsion gels. It will provide a novel predictive framework for designing tailored-texture foods, moving beyond empirical formulation. The findings will advance the fundamental colloid science of biocomposite interfaces in food matrices.
proposals.novelty: While bulk gel properties of protein-starch composites are well-studied, applying direct interfacial rheology—a standard tool in soft matter physics—to these specific biopolymer systems to explain texture is novel. This bridges a critical gap between colloidal-scale interface mechanics and macroscopic food material science.

proposals.title: Utilizing Comminuted Porcine Myofibrillar Protein Gel Particles as Novel Pickering Stabilizers: Structure, Emulsifying Properties, and Stability
proposals.motivation: Pickering emulsions stabilized by food-grade particles offer superior stability against coalescence. However, conventional protein particles often lack the structural robustness needed for long-term stability. Pre-2026 research by Youling Xiong and others has extensively characterized protein gel networks and composite gels, but the inverse concept—using pre-formed, comminuted protein gels as particulate emulsifiers—remains underexplored. This approach could create a new class of stabilizers with tunable interfacial and textural properties derived from the parent gel matrix.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that comminuted particles derived from porcine myofibrillar protein (MPP) gels, with their defined network structure and surface properties, can effectively stabilize oil-in-water Pickering emulsions, and that the emulsifying performance will be dictated by the particle size, surface hydrophobicity, and rheological properties of the parent gel.
proposals.method: First, MPP gels will be prepared under varying conditions (pH, ionic strength, heating regimes) informed by prior work on gel formation, then comminuted into microparticles via controlled shear and sieving. Particle size, zeta potential, and surface hydrophobicity will be characterized. These gel particles will be used to prepare oil-in-water emulsions. Emulsion stability will be assessed via droplet size distribution, creaming index, and microscopy over 14 days. Rheological and textural analyses of both the parent gels and the resulting emulsions will be performed to establish structure-function relationships, linking gel network properties (e.g., water-immobilizing capacity) to emulsifying activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel methodology for creating Pickering stabilizers from pre-formed protein gels, expanding the toolbox for designing structured emulsions. It will provide fundamental insights into how the macroscopic properties of a protein gel translate to the functionality of its particulate derivatives at fluid interfaces. The findings could lead to new applications in food systems, such as reduced-fat formulations or delivery systems, leveraging the dual functionality of gel particles as both stabilizers and texture modifiers.
proposals.novelty: While composite gels of proteins and polysaccharides have been studied, the direct use of comminuted, pure protein gel particles as Pickering stabilizers is a novel inversion of the typical gelation-in-emulsion paradigm. This approach intentionally decouples gel network formation from emulsification, allowing independent optimization of each process.

proposals.title: Disassembly Kinetics of Flaxseed Protein-Mung Bean Starch Composite Gels Under Simulated Gastrointestinal Digestion: The Role of Astragalus Polysaccharide
proposals.motivation: Composite protein-starch gels are promising for dysphagia foods and controlled nutrient delivery, but their functional efficacy depends on their breakdown behavior during digestion, which governs texture perception and nutrient release. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Xiong's own work, has focused on gel formation and static properties, leaving a critical gap in understanding the dynamic structural disassembly under physiologically relevant conditions. This knowledge is essential for designing next-generation foods with tailored digestion rates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the inclusion of Astragalus polysaccharide (APS) in flaxseed protein-mung bean starch composite gels will modulate the kinetics of gel disassembly during simulated digestion, leading to a more gradual breakdown and altered protein hydrolysis profile compared to control gels without APS.
proposals.method: We will fabricate composite gels with varying APS levels (0%, 0.5%, 1.0% w/w) based on established protocols. A standardized static in vitro digestion model (INFOGEST 2.0) will be employed to simulate oral, gastric, and intestinal phases. Gel breakdown will be tracked using time-resolved small-deformation rheology (loss tangent, G' and G") and particle size analysis (laser diffraction). Concurrently, the degree of protein hydrolysis (OPA assay) and starch hydrolysis (DNS method) will be quantified at each phase to link structural changes to nutrient release kinetics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct link between composite gel formulation, specifically APS addition, and its dynamic disintegration during digestion. It will provide novel mechanistic insights into how non-starch polysaccharides modify the digestion trajectory of protein-starch networks. The findings will offer a scientific basis for formulating gels with targeted textural stability and nutrient delivery profiles for specific dietary needs.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies characterized gel formation and initial properties, this proposal uniquely applies a kinetic, multi-phase digestion framework to directly monitor the real-time structural disassembly of a specific composite gel system, integrating rheological and biochemical analyses to deconstruct the breakdown process.

proposals.title: Dose-Dependent Modulation of Myofibrillar Protein Gel Properties by Controlled Oxidation: A Re-evaluation of Texture and Water-Holding Capacity
proposals.motivation: Oxidation of muscle proteins is widely considered detrimental to gelation, leading to inferior texture and water loss in meat products. This assumption, however, is largely based on studies using high, non-specific oxidation levels. In the context of developing clean-label and functional foods, a nuanced understanding of how controlled, specific oxidation conditions affect protein network formation is critically needed to challenge this oversimplified view.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mild, controlled oxidation of myofibrillar proteins can induce specific, beneficial cross-linking that enhances gel network density and homogeneity, thereby improving final gel hardness and water-holding capacity compared to both native and severely oxidized proteins.
proposals.method: We will use a hydroxyl radical-generating system (FeCl3/H2O2/ascorbate) to create a gradient of oxidation levels in porcine myofibrillar protein isolates. Gelation will be induced via a standard heat treatment. Gel texture (hardness, springiness) will be analyzed via texture profile analysis, and water-holding capacity via a centrifugal method. Microstructural changes will be characterized using confocal laser scanning microscopy and low-field NMR to assess water mobility and distribution. Protein cross-linking and carbonyl formation will be quantified via SDS-PAGE and spectrophotometric assays to correlate specific oxidation markers with functional outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, dose-response framework linking specific oxidative modifications to gel functionality, moving beyond the binary 'oxidation is bad' paradigm. It will offer practical insights for modulating protein gel texture through controlled oxidative processing, relevant for meat science and the design of dysphagia-friendly or high-protein foods.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the prevailing assumption by systematically testing the hypothesis that low-level oxidation can be a functional tool, not just a spoilage mechanism, for tailoring protein gel properties, an idea not thoroughly explored in pre-2026 literature on muscle protein gels.

proposals.title: Identification and Quantification of Specific Protein Oxidation Products in Myofibrillar Protein Gels Induced by Iron and Myoglobin Oxidative Systems
proposals.motivation: Oxidation is a critical factor influencing the gelation properties of muscle proteins, directly impacting texture, water-holding capacity, and product quality. While iron-oxidase (IOS) and myoglobin-oxidase (MOS) systems are known to induce protein oxidation, the specific carbonyl compounds (e.g., α-aminoadipic semialdehyde, γ-glutamic semialdehyde) and cross-links (e.g., disulfides, dityrosine, lysinoalanine) formed in myofibrillar protein gels remain poorly characterized. A detailed molecular-level understanding is essential to rationally control gel functionality in meat and composite gel products.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the IOS and MOS systems generate distinct profiles of specific protein carbonyls and cross-links during myofibrillar protein gelation, and that these specific oxidation products correlate directly with measurable changes in gel rheological and water-immobilizing properties.
proposals.method: Porcine myofibrillar protein will be isolated and subjected to controlled oxidation via IOS (FeCl3/H2O2/ascorbate) and MOS (metmyoglobin/H2O2) systems during heat-induced gelation. Specific protein carbonyls will be derivatized with 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine (DNPH) and quantified via HPLC, while targeted cross-links (dityrosine, disulfides) will be analyzed using fluorescence spectroscopy and SDS-PAGE under reducing/non-reducing conditions. Advanced techniques like LC-MS/MS will be employed for precise identification of adducts like γ-glutamic semialdehyde. Gel properties will be characterized in parallel using rheology and water-holding capacity measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive chemical fingerprint of protein oxidation products generated by IOS and MOS in a gelling system, moving beyond generic carbonyl assays. It will establish direct structure-function relationships between specific molecular modifications and the macroscopic properties of protein gels, offering a mechanistic basis for optimizing gel quality in muscle foods and protein-starch composites.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies (including the researcher's own) correlate general oxidation levels with gel properties, this proposal uniquely focuses on identifying the specific molecular species responsible, linking distinct oxidative pathways (IOS vs. MOS) to their unique chemical signatures and functional consequences in a gel network.

proposals.title: Optimizing Myofibrillar Protein Gel Networks: A Synergistic Approach Using Controlled Oxidant-Antioxidant Blends
proposals.motivation: In meat and protein-based food products, controlled protein oxidation is a double-edged sword. Mild oxidation promotes disulfide bonding and gel network formation, enhancing texture and water-holding capacity, but excessive oxidation leads to protein fragmentation, textural losses, and off-flavors. Current pre-2026 strategies often use single oxidants or antioxidants, lacking the precision to fine-tune the oxidative environment for optimal gel properties, especially in composite systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific blends of oxidizing agents (e.g., hydrogen peroxide, lipoxygenase) with mild pro-oxidants or low-dose antioxidants (e.g., eugenol, ascorbic acid at pro-oxidant levels) will create a synergistic, controlled oxidative environment that maximizes myofibrillar protein cross-linking for gel strength while mitigating protein degradation and textural defects.
proposals.method: Using porcine myofibrillar protein (MPI) as a base model, we will systematically test gel formation with individual and blended oxidative systems. A central composite design will optimize concentrations of a primary oxidant (e.g., H2O2), a secondary pro-oxidant (e.g., FeCl3/ascorbate), and a phenolic compound (eugenol, based on prior work). Gel properties will be characterized via dynamic rheology (gel strength, G'), texture profile analysis, water immobilization (LF-NMR), and protein network microstructure (SEM, SDS-PAGE for cross-linking). The synergistic effect will be quantified by comparing observed results to predicted additive values.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel formulation principle for tailoring protein gel textures by deliberately balancing oxidative pathways. It will provide a quantitative framework for using antioxidant-prooxidant blends as precise tools, rather than mere inhibitors, in protein gel design. The findings will directly benefit the development of high-quality structured foods, including meat analogs and dysphagia-friendly gels, by improving texture control.
proposals.novelty: While the individual effects of oxidants or antioxidants on proteins are known, the deliberate design of synergistic oxidant-antioxidant blends to *steer* and *optimize*, rather than simply inhibit, the gelation process is a novel and non-obvious approach. This moves beyond single-ingredient studies to a systems-level manipulation of redox chemistry for textural engineering.

proposals.title: Systematic mapping of oxidative modification on myofibrillar protein gelation: Rheological, structural, and water-binding responses across a full pH gradient
proposals.motivation: Oxidation is a critical factor influencing protein gelation, particularly in meat processing, but its effects are highly pH-dependent. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Xiong's work on eugenol, has focused on specific pH conditions, leaving a significant knowledge gap regarding how oxidation modulates gel networks across the full pH spectrum relevant to food formulation and muscle food quality. A systematic map is needed to predict and control gel properties in diverse food matrices, from acidic gels to neutral processed meats.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that oxidation will have a non-linear, pH-dependent effect on myofibrillar protein gelation, where moderate oxidation enhances gel strength and water immobilization at pH values above 6.0 by promoting disulfide cross-linking, but exacerbates protein aggregation and weakens gel structure at pH values below 6.0 by dominating hydrophobic interactions over electrostatic repulsion.
proposals.method: Porcine myofibrillar protein will be subjected to controlled oxidation using a hydroxyl radical-generating system (FeCl3/ascorbate/H2O2) at varying concentrations. Gelation will be induced by heating at a series of fixed pH values (pH 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 5.5, 6.0, 6.5, 7.0, 8.0). The response will be quantified using dynamic rheology for gelation kinetics, texture profile analysis for gel strength, low-field NMR for water mobility, and SDS-PAGE with specific staining for sulfhydryl/disulfide content and protein polymerization to elucidate network formation mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a comprehensive 'map' correlating oxidation degree, pH, and final gel properties, providing a predictive framework for food scientists. It will clarify the conflicting roles of oxidation in gel enhancement versus deterioration, directly informing the formulation of protein gels with tailored textures for applications ranging from dysphagia foods to high-quality meat products.
proposals.novelty: While oxidation and pH effects are studied in isolation, systematically coupling controlled oxidation with a full pH gradient to decouple and model their interactive effects on gelation is novel. This approach moves beyond single-condition studies to create a fundamental, transferable understanding of the protein network-forming landscape.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning-Guided Inverse Design Approach for Antioxidants that Protect Myofibrillar Protein Gel Texture from Oxidation-Induced Degradation
proposals.motivation: In meat and protein-based food gels, lipid oxidation compromises texture by promoting excessive, disorganized protein aggregation, leading to weakened network strength and water loss. While antioxidants can mitigate oxidation, they often indiscriminately inhibit all protein-protein interactions, including the beneficial cross-linking crucial for early gel network formation. There is a critical need to identify antioxidant molecules that specifically protect against texture-degrading oxidative pathways without interfering with essential gelation chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by modeling the relationship between antioxidant molecular descriptors and their differential effects on oxidative protection versus gelation inhibition, we can inversely design or identify antioxidant compounds that selectively preserve gel texture under oxidative stress.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-stage computational and experimental pipeline. First, we will curate a dataset from literature (pre-2026) on the effects of various phenolic and other antioxidants on myofibrillar protein gel properties (e.g., hardness, water holding capacity, storage modulus) and oxidation markers. Using this dataset, we will train a multi-task neural network model (e.g., a graph convolutional network for molecular input) to predict both 'texture protection score' and 'gelation inhibition score'. The model will then be used in an inverse design loop using a generative model (like a variational autoencoder) or a search algorithm (like Bayesian optimization) over chemical space to propose candidate antioxidant structures that maximize the protection-inhibition differential. Top candidates will be validated experimentally using a porcine myofibrillar protein gel system, assessing texture, rheology, and protein carbonyl formation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational framework for the targeted design of food-grade antioxidants, moving beyond trial-and-error. It will provide specific antioxidant candidates and establish a quantitative structure-property relationship (QSPR) for antioxidant functionality in complex protein gel matrices, directly addressing quality preservation in processed meats and protein gels.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in framing antioxidant selection as an inverse design problem specifically for gel texture protection, integrating multi-task prediction of conflicting functionalities (anti-oxidation vs. non-interference with gelation) using pre-2026 machine learning methods, which is distinct from conventional screening based solely on antioxidant capacity.

proposals.title: Controlled oxidation by glucose oxidase and xanthine oxidase systems to enhance the gelation and textural properties of flaxseed and mung bean protein composite gels
proposals.motivation: The demand for high-quality plant-based foods, especially for specialized diets like dysphagia, is growing. Plant proteins often exhibit inferior gelation and water-holding properties compared to animal proteins, limiting their texture and functionality. Pre-2026 research has shown that enzymatic oxidation systems (e.g., glucose oxidase, xanthine oxidase) can induce protein cross-linking in muscle proteins, but their systematic application to modulate plant protein composite gel networks remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that controlled oxidative treatment using glucose oxidase (GO) and xanthine oxidase (XO) systems will induce covalent cross-linking in flaxseed and mung bean protein composites, thereby strengthening the gel network, improving water immobilization, and enhancing rheological and textural properties suitable for soft-solid foods.
proposals.method: Flaxseed protein isolate (FPI) and mung bean protein isolate (MPI) will be extracted and combined to form composite gels. The gels will be treated with two enzymatic oxidative systems: 1) Glucose oxidase (GO) with glucose, and 2) Xanthine oxidase (XO) with hypoxanthine. Gel properties will be characterized using small-deformation rheology (gel strength, G'), large-deformation texture analysis (hardness, springiness), water-holding capacity, and microstructure via scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The cross-linking extent will be assessed by SDS-PAGE and quantification of dityrosine formation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, clean-label method to engineer the texture of plant protein gels, directly addressing functionality gaps in meat analogs and dysphagia foods. It will provide a mechanistic understanding of how specific oxidative pathways modify plant protein interactions in composite systems, offering a targeted alternative to chemical cross-linkers.
proposals.novelty: While IOS/MOS systems are known for meat protein modification, their targeted use to specifically improve the gel network of plant protein-starch composites, particularly flaxseed and mung bean, is novel. The approach combines the researcher's expertise in composite gels and enzymatic activity to create a new strategy for plant-based texture design.

proposals.title: Comparative Impact of Chemical Oxidation on the Thermal Stability and Gelation Properties of Diverse Plant Proteins
proposals.motivation: Plant protein oxidation, induced by processing and storage, is known to alter functional properties like gelation and emulsification, crucial for food texture. However, preliminary observations suggest soy protein isolate (SPI) may uniquely resist thermal destabilization upon oxidation, a potential advantage for ingredient formulation. This project will systematically test if this resilience is a universal trait across major plant proteins or if SPI is an outlier, addressing a significant gap in understanding protein structure-function relationships under oxidative stress.
proposals.hypothesis: The thermal stability of plant proteins is not uniformly resistant to oxidation; proteins with distinct structural compositions (e.g., globulins vs. albumins) or from different botanical sources (e.g., flaxseed, pea, mung bean) will exhibit varied thermal denaturation responses to controlled oxidative treatment, contrary to the assumed universal stability observed in SPI.
proposals.method: We will subject purified proteins (soy 11S/7S, pea legumin, flaxseed protein, mung bean protein) to a hydroxyl radical-generating system (FeCl3/ascorbate/H2O2) for controlled oxidation. Thermal stability will be assessed via differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) to measure denaturation temperature (Td) and enthalpy (ΔH). Gelation properties of oxidized proteins will be evaluated using small-amplitude oscillatory rheology to monitor storage modulus (G') development during heating and cooling, complemented by textural profile analysis and water-holding capacity measurements of formed gels.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a comparative framework for the oxidative stability of plant protein functionality, directly informing ingredient selection for processed foods. It will elucidate whether SPI's thermal resilience is a generalizable model or an exception, providing critical data for predicting protein performance in complex food matrices and under harsh processing conditions.
proposals.novelty: While prior research (including the PI's work on composite gels) examines oxidation effects on specific proteins or gel networks, a systematic, comparative study testing the universality of SPI's oxidation-resistant thermal stability across diverse plant protein classes is novel. The integration of harsher, controlled chemical oxidation with detailed thermo-rheological profiling moves beyond common mild treatments.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Impact of Controlled Protein Oxidation on the Water-Holding Capacity and Syneresis of Starch-Protein Composite Gels
proposals.motivation: Protein oxidation, often induced by processing or storage, is a major factor degrading the functional properties of proteins in food matrices. For composite gels designed for applications like dysphagia foods, where water-holding capacity (WHC) is critical for texture and safety, the specific impact of oxidation on the gel network's ability to immobilize water is poorly quantified. Understanding this relationship is essential for formulating stable, high-quality protein-based foods with consistent performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that controlled oxidation of the protein component (e.g., flaxseed or muscle protein) will induce specific structural modifications that weaken the composite gel network, leading to a quantifiable, dose-dependent decrease in water-holding capacity and an increase in syneresis.
proposals.method: Flaxseed protein isolate and mung bean starch will form the base composite gel system. Protein oxidation will be induced in vitro using a FeCl3/H2O2/ascorbate hydroxyl radical-generating system with varying H2O2 concentrations (0-10 mM) and incubation times. Gelation will be induced via thermal treatment. WHC will be measured by a centrifugal method and low-field NMR to assess water distribution and mobility. Syneresis will be quantified gravimetrically after storage. Rheological properties (storage/loss moduli) and microstructure (CLSM, SEM) will be correlated with WHC data to establish structure-function relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative model linking oxidation degree to functional deterioration in composite gels, providing concrete formulation guidelines to mitigate syneresis. It will advance the fundamental understanding of how oxidative protein modifications specifically compromise water immobilization within a multi-component biopolymer network.
proposals.novelty: While oxidation's effect on pure protein gels is studied, its systematic impact on the water-binding functionality of *composite* starch-protein gels, a critical system for tailored food textures, remains unexplored. This proposal uniquely focuses on quantifying syneresis, a direct quality defect, as a primary metric.

proposals.title: Controlled Oxidation of Flaxseed-Mung Bean Protein-Starch Composite Gels: A Response Surface Methodology Approach to Optimize Network Elasticity and Water Immobilization
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research shows that moderate protein oxidation can enhance gelation by promoting disulfide bonding and hydrophobic interactions, but excessive oxidation damages amino acids and impairs network formation. For composite gels, such as those based on plant proteins and starches for dysphagia foods, the precise oxidation 'sweet spot' that maximizes desirable textural properties like elasticity without causing protein damage remains undefined. Optimizing this balance is critical for developing superior, texture-controlled food products with clean labels.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a flaxseed protein and mung bean starch composite gel, there exists a specific, quantifiable degree of oxidation—induced by a controlled oxidizing agent—that will maximize gel elasticity and water-holding capacity by optimally promoting beneficial protein cross-links while minimizing the formation of detrimental protein carbonyls and aggregates.
proposals.method: Flaxseed protein isolate and mung bean starch will be used to form composite gels. Oxidation levels will be precisely controlled using hydrogen peroxide at varying concentrations (0-20 mM) and incubation times. A central composite design within Response Surface Methodology will model the effects of oxidation level, protein:starch ratio, and heating regime on key responses. Responses will include rheological storage modulus (G'), fracture strain, water immobilization, and quantification of protein carbonyls and free sulfhydryl groups to directly correlate oxidative damage with network properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative model linking specific oxidation degrees to the structural and functional properties of a plant-based composite gel. It will provide a methodological framework for intentionally using controlled oxidation as a tool, rather than an uncontrolled variable, in protein gel design. The findings will directly inform the development of optimized textured foods for specific nutritional applications, such as dysphagia diets.
proposals.novelty: While oxidation's role in muscle protein gels is studied, its systematic optimization in plant protein-starch composite gels, particularly for elasticity enhancement, is novel. The integration of chemical oxidation metrics (carbonyls, SH groups) with rheological and textural optimization in a single RSM model for such a composite system represents a significant advance over existing phenomenological studies.

proposals.title: Mapping Protein Phase Distribution and Molecular Interactions in Plant-Protein Composite Gels using Confocal Raman Microscopy
proposals.motivation: Composite gels of plant proteins and polysaccharides are promising for sustainable, dysphagia-friendly foods, but their macroscopic properties are dictated by poorly understood microstructural heterogeneity. Pre-2026, conventional microscopy lacks the chemical specificity to distinguish protein phases from polysaccharides and to probe molecular interactions (e.g., hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic contacts) at the sub-micron scale, limiting rational gel design.
