proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that confocal Raman microscopy will reveal distinct, spatially resolved protein-rich and polysaccharide-rich domains within composite gels, and that the intensity and shift of key Raman bands (e.g., Amide I, C-H stretching) will correlate with local molecular interactions governing gel strength and water immobilization.
proposals.method: We will fabricate composite gels from mung bean starch and flaxseed protein, incorporating Astragalus polysaccharide (APS) as per our prior work. Gel microstructure will be mapped using a confocal Raman microscope (e.g., WITec alpha300 R) with a 532 nm laser. We will acquire hyperspectral image cubes, then apply multivariate curve resolution (MCR) analysis to chemically unmix the spectra and generate false-color maps of protein, starch, and APS distribution. Specific band analysis (e.g., Amide I peak position for protein secondary structure, 480 cm-1 band for starch conformation) will be performed on spectra extracted from identified phase domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, label-free visualization of phase-separated protein networks in plant-based composite gels, linking spatial chemical composition to macroscopic rheological and textural data. It will establish a spectroscopic imaging framework to quantify how bioactive compounds (like APS) modulate local molecular environments, advancing beyond descriptive microstructure to predictive, interaction-driven gel design.
proposals.novelty: While Raman spectroscopy has been used in bulk for proteins, its application as a high-resolution imaging tool to spatially resolve intermolecular interactions within the complex, hydrated network of a composite food gel is novel. This directly addresses a key gap in understanding structure-function relationships in multi-component soft solids.

proposals.title: Molecular-scale interactions between oxidized porcine myosin and purified soy β-conglycinin: mechanisms governing composite gel network formation
proposals.motivation: Composite gels of muscle and plant proteins are critical for developing sustainable, high-quality hybrid meat products. However, the fundamental protein-protein interactions driving network formation, especially under oxidative stress common in processing, are poorly understood at the molecular level. Pre-2026 work, including Dr. Xiong's on eugenol and protein gels, highlights the need to move beyond empirical composite studies to mechanistic, component-resolved investigations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that controlled oxidation of myosin will create specific covalent and non-covalent interaction sites that synergistically, rather than competitively, bind with soy β-conglycinin, leading to a denser, more elastic composite gel network than either protein alone.
proposals.method: We will isolate porcine myosin and soy β-conglycinin to >95% purity. Myosin will be subjected to a controlled oxidation system (Fe³⁺/H₂O₂/ascorbate) to mimic processing stress. Interactions will be studied using isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) to quantify binding thermodynamics and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) to measure association/dissociation kinetics. Small-deformation rheology and confocal laser scanning microscopy will be used to correlate specific interaction parameters (Kd, ΔH) with the viscoelastic properties and microstructure of gels formed from the purified binary mixtures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative structure-interaction-property relationship for a key muscle-plant protein pair, providing a foundational model for rational design of composite protein gels. It will directly explain how oxidation, often considered detrimental, can be harnessed to engineer specific interactions in hybrid systems, advancing the science of protein co-gelation.
proposals.novelty: While composite gels are widely studied empirically, this proposal is novel in applying rigorous biophysical binding assays (ITC, SPR) to deconstruct the specific molecular interactions between purified, oxidized myosin and a defined soy globulin, moving from phenomenological to mechanistic understanding.

proposals.title: Rational Design of Dual-Function Antioxidants for Tailored Oxidation in Myofibrillar Protein-Soy Protein Isolate Composite Gels
proposals.motivation: In composite meat analog gels, oxidation must be carefully managed. Excessive oxidation degrades myofibrillar protein (MP) quality, while controlled oxidation can promote beneficial disulfide bonding with plant proteins like soy protein isolate (SPI), strengthening the gel network. Current antioxidants broadly inhibit all oxidation, potentially weakening the composite gel structure by preventing these crucial SPI-MP interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific antioxidants can be selected or designed to selectively protect sensitive myofibrillar protein sites (e.g., methionine, histidine) from detrimental oxidation while permitting controlled, beneficial oxidation (e.g., sulfhydryl-disulfide interchange) at the interface with SPI, thereby optimizing both protein protection and gel network formation.
proposals.method: We will employ a computational-experimental inverse design strategy. First, molecular docking and molecular dynamics simulations (using AutoDock Vina and GROMACS) will screen a library of natural phenolic antioxidants (e.g., from the cited eugenol work) for binding affinity to critical MP oxidation sites versus SPI interaction domains. Promising candidates will be experimentally tested in a MP-SPI composite gel model. We will measure protein carbonyls (detrimental oxidation), sulfhydryl loss/disulfide formation (beneficial cross-linking), and correlate these with rheological properties (gel strength, water-holding capacity) and microstructure (SEM).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel framework for 'precision antioxidant' selection in protein composites, moving beyond blanket inhibition. It will provide specific structure-activity rules for antioxidants that balance protection and promotion of cross-linking, directly contributing to the design of improved-texture hybrid meat products and dysphagia foods.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the standard problem from 'how to stop oxidation' to 'how to design an antioxidant to permit specific, beneficial oxidation,' and integrates computational screening specifically for differential protection within a composite protein system, a concept not explored in prior composite gel studies.

proposals.title: Dose-dependent antioxidant effects of eugenol on the gelation, oxidation, and texture of a composite meat-muscle protein and starch gel system
proposals.motivation: Antioxidants like eugenol are used to inhibit lipid oxidation in muscle foods, but their efficacy is often studied in isolated protein systems. In complex, multi-component foods containing proteins, starches, and lipids, interactions can significantly alter antioxidant activity, making dosage optimization from simple models ineffective for practical formulation. This gap hinders the development of clean-label, shelf-stable foods with optimal texture and nutritional quality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a composite gel system mimicking a real food matrix, the dose-response relationship of eugenol's antioxidant effect will be non-linear and modulated by its concurrent impact on myofibrillar protein gelation and starch-protein network formation.
proposals.method: A model composite gel will be formulated with porcine myofibrillar protein, mung bean starch, and a defined lipid fraction. Eugenol will be incorporated at 0, 0.01%, 0.02%, 0.05%, and 0.1% (w/w). Gel properties will be characterized using dynamic rheology for gel strength, texture profile analysis, and water-holding capacity. Lipid oxidation will be monitored during chilled storage via thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS). The dose-dependent effects will be modeled using response surface methodology to identify optimal dosages for balancing antioxidant protection and desired gel texture.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical framework for formulating complex, gel-based foods with optimized natural antioxidant use, directly linking dosage to functional and oxidative stability outcomes. It will advance the understanding of how polyphenols like eugenol function in multi-phase food matrices, moving beyond single-component studies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating the dose-dependent study of a bioactive compound (eugenol) into a composite protein-starch gel system, explicitly investigating the trade-off between its antioxidant activity and its known concentration-dependent influence on protein gel network formation, which has not been systematically explored for practical food design.

proposals.title: Impact of Hempseed Protein Hydrolysate Incorporation on the Sensory Profile and Gel Network Properties of a Model Meat Analogue System
proposals.motivation: Hempseed protein is gaining traction as a sustainable, plant-based protein source, but its inherent bitterness and grassy aroma, often intensified by hydrolysis, pose significant barriers to consumer acceptance in fortified foods. Pre-2026 research on protein gels, including Dr. Xiong's work on myofibrillar and composite gels, focuses heavily on texture and water-holding, with sensory impact often assessed as a secondary outcome. A systematic investigation linking specific hydrolysate characteristics to sensory perception in a structured gel matrix is needed to guide product formulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that controlled enzymatic hydrolysis of hempseed protein will generate specific peptide profiles that directly influence the bitterness and aroma intensity of a model meat analogue gel, and that these sensory attributes will correlate with measurable changes in the gel's microstructure and water-immobilizing properties.
proposals.method: Hempseed protein isolate will be hydrolyzed using Alcalase and Flavourzyme under controlled conditions (time, enzyme-substrate ratio) to produce hydrolysates with varying degrees of hydrolysis. These will be incorporated into a model composite gel system based on plant proteins and starch, building on Dr. Xiong's expertise with mung bean starch-flaxseed protein gels. Gel properties will be characterized via texture profile analysis, rheology, and water-holding capacity. Sensory impact will be quantified via descriptive analysis by a trained panel and correlated with chemical analyses (e.g., HPLC for bitter peptide profiles, GC-MS for volatile compounds).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct structure-sensory-property relationship for hempseed protein hydrolysates in a gel food matrix. It will provide formulators with targeted strategies to mitigate off-flavors while leveraging the functional benefits of hydrolysis, such as improved emulsifying activity, thereby advancing the development of palatable, protein-fortified foods for health-conscious and dysphagia diets.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have examined the functional properties of protein hydrolysates, this proposal uniquely integrates detailed sensory descriptive analysis with microstructural and rheological characterization of the composite gel network to deconvolute the specific contribution of hydrolysate-derived peptides to both taste and texture, a non-obvious linkage in pre-2026 plant protein gel research.

proposals.title: Controlled Softening of Myofibrillar Protein Gels by Phytophenols for Novel Textured Meat Analogs: A Rheological, Microstructural, and Sensory Study
proposals.motivation: Current research on protein-polyphenol gels predominantly focuses on enhancing gel strength, overlooking potential applications where a softer, more ductile texture is desirable, such as in plant-based meat analogs mimicking tender or emulsified meat products. The pre-2026 work of this researcher demonstrates that eugenol can weaken myofibrillar protein gels, presenting an opportunity to intentionally exploit this softening phenomenon. This challenges the prevailing paradigm that stronger gels are universally superior, opening a new design space for tailored food textures.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific phytophenols (e.g., eugenol, catechin) can be used as deliberate, concentration-dependent modifiers to create a spectrum of myofibrillar protein gel textures, from strong and elastic to soft and spreadable, by selectively interfering with protein network cross-linking without causing complete network failure.
proposals.method: We will prepare porcine myofibrillar protein gels with varying concentrations of eugenol and catechin, building directly on the researcher's prior methodology. Gel properties will be characterized using small-amplitude oscillatory shear rheology to map viscoelastic moduli, texture profile analysis for hardness and cohesiveness, and confocal laser scanning microscopy to visualize network microstructure. These physical data will be correlated with sensory analysis of model food systems (e.g., structured plant-protein patties) to establish structure-property-sensation relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational framework for using phytophenols as targeted texture-softening agents in protein gel design, moving beyond their traditional roles as antioxidants or mere gel inhibitors. It will provide concrete formulation guidelines for developing novel meat analog textures that prioritize tenderness and mouthfeel over extreme firmness, directly addressing texture diversification needs in the alternative protein sector.
proposals.novelty: While polyphenols are known to sometimes weaken protein gels, systematically harnessing this effect as a primary design tool for creating desirable soft-solid textures in meat analogs is a novel and counter-intuitive approach. It repurposes a typically undesirable interaction into a controlled structuring strategy.

proposals.title: Competitive and Synergistic Binding of Multi-Phenol Mixtures to Myofibrillar and Sarcoplasmic Proteins: Impact on Gel Network Formation and Water Immobilization
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research shows that single phytophenols, like eugenol, significantly alter muscle protein gelation, a critical process for meat product texture and functionality. However, real food systems and functional ingredients often contain complex mixtures of phenols (e.g., from herb extracts) and multiple protein types, creating a knowledge gap in predicting their combined effects on gel properties. Understanding the competitive binding and functional interactions in these multi-component systems is essential for rationally designing clean-label, protein-based foods with tailored textures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in mixtures containing myofibrillar and sarcoplasmic proteins, different phytophenols (e.g., eugenol, catechin, quercetin) will exhibit competitive and structure-dependent binding affinities, leading to non-additive, synergistic, or antagonistic effects on protein aggregation, gel network microstructure, and final rheological/textural properties.
proposals.method: We will extract porcine myofibrillar protein (MPI) and sarcoplasmic protein (SP) fractions. Binary and ternary phenol mixtures (eugenol, catechin, quercetin) at varying ratios will be introduced to individual and combined protein systems. Gel formation will be induced by heating. Interactions will be characterized via fluorescence quenching and isothermal titration calorimetry. Gel properties will be assessed using dynamic rheometry, texture profile analysis, low-field NMR for water mobility, and confocal laser scanning microscopy for network visualization. A multivariate statistical model (e.g., PCA and response surface methodology) will decode the contribution of each component.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a foundational mechanistic framework for predicting gel behavior in complex, multi-phenol/protein systems, moving beyond single-component studies. It will deliver practical insights for formulating meat analogs and dysphagia foods using natural phenolic blends to precisely control texture and water retention, aligning with clean-label trends.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by the researcher studied single phenols or composite gels with polysaccharides, this proposal systematically investigates the under-explored competitive binding landscape and functional outcomes in multi-phenol, multi-protein muscle systems, a critical step towards real-world formulation complexity.

proposals.title: Controlled enzymatic hydrolysis of flaxseed protein to decouple and prioritize gelation functionality over bioactivity for dysphagia food applications
proposals.motivation: Protein hydrolysates are primarily valued for bioactive peptides, but their non-bioactive functional properties, like gelation, are crucial for structuring foods, especially for specialized diets like dysphagia. Pre-2026 work on composite gels (e.g., mung bean starch-flaxseed protein) shows promise, but the direct impact of hydrolysis parameters on the gel network, independent of bioactivity, is poorly understood. This limits the targeted design of protein ingredients for texture-modified foods.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that controlled, limited enzymatic hydrolysis of flaxseed protein will generate peptide mixtures with superior gelation and water-immobilizing properties, and that this functionality can be optimized independently of the degree of hydrolysis typically associated with high bioactivity.
proposals.method: Flaxseed protein isolate will be hydrolyzed using Alcalase under controlled conditions (time, enzyme-substrate ratio) to produce hydrolysates with varying degrees of hydrolysis (DH). Gelation properties will be rigorously characterized using rheology (small-amplitude oscillatory shear), texture profile analysis, and water-holding capacity measurements, following methods from recent composite gel studies. The gel network microstructure will be analyzed using scanning electron microscopy. Bioactivity (specifically ACE-inhibitory activity) will be assayed in parallel to explicitly track its correlation, or lack thereof, with the gel functionality metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a processing framework for producing functional protein hydrolysates where gelation is the primary design target, not a byproduct of bioactivity generation. It will provide clear structure-function relationships for flaxseed protein hydrolysate gels, contributing directly to the development of plant-based, texture-controlled foods for dysphagia management.
proposals.novelty: While hydrolysis is known to modify functionality, the intentional decoupling and prioritization of gelation over the more commonly sought bioactivity in oilseed protein hydrolysates is a novel conceptual and applied approach. The systematic correlation of DH with both gel properties and a key bioactivity marker within the same study is also novel.

proposals.title: Optimization of Enzymatic Hydrolysis for Targeted Bioactive Peptide Release from Flaxseed-Mung Bean Protein and Its Impact on Composite Gel Properties
proposals.motivation: Bioactive peptides from plant proteins offer significant health benefits, such as antihypertensive and antioxidant activities, but their production is inefficient and non-specific. Pre-2026 work on composite gels for dysphagia food focuses on physical properties, neglecting the potential to engineer these gels as functional food matrices with embedded bioactive peptides. This research addresses the gap by linking targeted protein hydrolysis to both peptide bioactivity and the material properties of the resulting protein-starch composite gels.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific, optimized degree of hydrolysis (DH) for flaxseed protein will maximize the yield of target bioactive peptides while concurrently modifying protein functionality to produce composite gels with enhanced textural and water-immobilizing properties suitable for dysphagia diets.
proposals.method: Flaxseed protein isolate will be hydrolyzed with Alcalase under controlled conditions (pH, temperature, enzyme-substrate ratio) to achieve a DH gradient (2-15%). Peptide profiles will be characterized via LC-MS/MS and screened in silico using the BIOPEP-UWM database for potential antihypertensive (ACE-inhibitory) and antioxidant sequences. Peptide activity will be validated with in vitro ACE inhibition and DPPH/ABTS assays. The hydrolysates will then be incorporated into mung bean starch composite gels, and their rheological (dynamic oscillatory), textural (TPA), and water-holding properties will be evaluated, following methods established in the researcher's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct methodology for producing plant protein hydrolysates enriched in specific bioactive peptides. It will provide a novel understanding of how targeted hydrolysis, aimed at bioactivity, functionally modifies proteins for gel network formation, contributing to the design of next-generation functional soft foods with defined health and textural attributes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating bioactive peptide discovery and validation with the material science of dysphagia food gels, moving beyond empirical hydrolysis optimization to a purpose-driven strategy that co-optimizes for bioactivity and matrix functionality.

proposals.title: Functional and Structural Performance of Porcine Myofibrillar Protein Hydrolysates in Composite Gels and Emulsions for Food and Cosmetic Applications
proposals.motivation: The valorization of meat processing by-products through enzymatic hydrolysis is a key strategy for sustainable protein recovery, generating bioactive peptides and functional ingredients. However, pre-2026 research often evaluates hydrolysates in isolation, not within complex product matrices where interactions with other biopolymers (e.g., starch, polysaccharides) critically determine final texture, stability, and bioactivity. A systematic evaluation in real formulations is needed to bridge the gap between lab-scale functionality and commercial application in functional foods and personal care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that controlled enzymatic hydrolysis of porcine myofibrillar protein will yield hydrolysates with tunable emulsifying and gelling properties, and that their incorporation into composite starch-protein-polysaccharide matrices will enhance gel strength, water immobilization, and emulsion stability compared to native proteins, while maintaining or improving bioactivity.
proposals.method: Porcine myofibrillar protein will be hydrolyzed using food-grade proteases (e.g., Alcalase, Flavourzyme) to varying degrees of hydrolysis (DH). Hydrolysate functionality (emulsifying activity, solubility, peptide profile) will be characterized. They will then be incorporated into two model systems: 1) A composite gel matrix of mung bean starch and flaxseed protein, with/without Astragalus polysaccharide, to assess rheological, textural, and water-holding properties. 2) An oil-in-water emulsion model for cosmetic creams to assess stability, droplet size, and rheology. Performance will be compared to native protein controls and correlated with DH and peptide profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical framework for formulating with meat protein hydrolysates in multi-component systems, directly informing the development of value-added foods (e.g., for dysphagia) and stable cosmetic emulsions. It will establish clear structure-function relationships between hydrolysis conditions, peptide characteristics, and performance in complex matrices, moving beyond single-ingredient studies.
proposals.novelty: While previous work by the researcher studied gel formation with eugenol or composite gels with intact proteins/polysaccharides, this proposal uniquely integrates protein hydrolysates—derived from the studied myofibrillar protein—into these complex networks to evaluate their dual role as functional ingredients and bioactive carriers in applied matrices.

proposals.title: Fractionation of porcine myofibrillar protein hydrolysates by dairy membrane processing: effects on gel network formation and emulsifying activity
proposals.motivation: Bioactive peptides from meat proteins, particularly myofibrillar proteins (MP), hold promise for enhancing food functionality and health benefits. However, uncontrolled hydrolysis produces a complex mixture of peptides with varied sizes and properties, which can detrimentally impact critical functional properties like gelation and emulsification. Pre-2026, there is a need for scalable, food-grade methods to separate hydrolysates by molecular weight to isolate fractions with targeted functionalities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting ultrafiltration (UF) and nanofiltration (NF) membrane cascades from whey processing will effectively fractionate MP hydrolysates by peptide size, and that specific molecular weight fractions will exhibit superior gel-forming and emulsifying properties compared to the unfractionated hydrolysate.
proposals.method: Porcine MP will be hydrolyzed with a food-grade protease (e.g., Alcalase) to a controlled degree. The hydrolysate will be fractionated using a sequential membrane filtration system: first with a 10 kDa UF membrane to separate large peptides/proteins, then the permeate with a 3 kDa UF membrane, and finally with a 1 kDa NF membrane. The resulting retentate fractions (R10k, R3k, R1k) and final permeate will be collected. Each fraction will be characterized for peptide profile (SDS-PAGE, HPLC) and assessed for gel properties (rheology, texture, water-holding) and emulsifying activity index (EAI).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel processing link between dairy and meat science, providing a practical method to tailor the functionality of meat protein hydrolysates. It will directly reveal the relationship between peptide size distribution from membrane fractionation and key techno-functional properties (gelation, emulsification), enabling the design of improved composite food matrices for applications like dysphagia foods or clean-label meat products.
proposals.novelty: While membrane fractionation is standard in dairy, its systematic application to tailor the gelation and emulsification of muscle protein hydrolysates is novel. This approach moves beyond simply producing hydrolysates to actively engineering their composition based on techniques proven in another protein system.

proposals.title: Controlled pH Refolding of Myofibrillar Proteins: A Novel Strategy to Engineer Gel Network Properties for Tailored Food Textures
proposals.motivation: The functional properties of protein gels, such as water-holding capacity, texture, and stability, are critical for food quality, especially in applications like dysphagia foods and meat analogs. Current protein gelation research, including Dr. Xiong's prior work, often focuses on additives or composite systems, treating the protein's initial state as a fixed variable. Systematically altering the protein's refolding pathway by varying pH presents an underexplored, fundamental lever to control final gel architecture and functionality.
proposals.hypothesis: Refolding porcine myofibrillar proteins under controlled acidic or alkaline conditions (e.g., pH 5.0, 9.0) prior to thermal gelation will induce distinct, non-native conformational states, leading to gels with significantly different rheological, textural, and water-immobilizing properties compared to gels from proteins refolded at neutral pH.
proposals.method: Porcine myofibrillar proteins will be first denatured in 6M urea. Refolding will be initiated by rapid dilution into buffers at pH 5.0, 7.0 (control), and 9.0, followed by dialysis. The structural states of refolded proteins will be characterized using circular dichroism and surface hydrophobicity measurements. These protein solutions will then be used to form heat-induced gels. Gel properties will be analyzed using small-deformation rheology (gel strength, viscoelasticity), texture profile analysis (hardness, springiness), and water-holding capacity measurements, following methodologies established in the researcher's recent composite gel work.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational link between protein refolding history and macroscopic gel functionality, moving beyond additive-based modification strategies. It will provide a novel, clean-label method to engineer food protein textures, potentially creating gels optimized for specific applications, such as firm meat analogs or soft, high-moisture dysphagia foods, without relying on polysaccharide composites.
proposals.novelty: While pH is known to affect gelation, the intentional use of non-neutral pH as a refolding condition to trap proteins in alternative functional states prior to gel network formation is a novel, inversion of the standard protocol. This approach directly targets the protein's conformational starting point, a variable largely held constant in prior composite and additive studies.

proposals.title: Precision Modulation of Myofibrillar Protein Gel Networks: Mapping the Synergistic Effects of Near-Physiological pH Shifts and Dietary Lipid Profiles
proposals.motivation: Current strategies to enhance protein gel functionality, such as for dysphagia foods or processed meats, often rely on harsh pH adjustments or non-physiological additives, which can compromise nutritional quality and consumer acceptance. Pre-2026 research shows that mild pH shifts can alter protein conformation and network formation, but the critical role of co-present dietary lipids, which vary widely in fatty acid composition, is poorly understood. A systematic investigation is needed to determine if functional benefits are universally achievable or are contingent on specific, biochemically compatible pH-lipid pairs.
proposals.hypothesis: The functional enhancement of myofibrillar protein gels by mild pH shifts is not universal but is critically dependent on the fatty acid profile of the co-formulated lipid, with specific pH-lipid combinations yielding synergistic improvements in gel network properties due to optimized protein-lipid interactions.
proposals.method: Porcine myofibrillar protein gels will be formulated with four distinct lipid sources (lard, tallow, olive oil, coconut oil) representing saturated, monounsaturated, and polyunsaturated profiles. Gels will be prepared under a matrix of mild pH conditions (pH 5.8, 6.2, 6.6, 7.0). A comprehensive analysis will be performed: rheology (small-amplitude oscillatory shear) to monitor gelation kinetics and final viscoelasticity; texture profile analysis for hardness and springiness; low-field NMR for water mobility and distribution; and confocal laser scanning microscopy to visualize the protein network and lipid droplet integration. Statistical modeling (e.g., response surface methodology) will map the interaction landscape.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a foundational map of pH-lipid interactions for protein gel functionality, moving the field towards precision formulation. It will identify tailored, mild processing conditions that maximize gel strength, water retention, and texture without extreme processing, directly applicable to designing next-generation muscle foods and dysphagia diets. The findings will establish a new principle: functional enhancement requires biochemical compatibility between the processing environment and the lipid matrix.
proposals.novelty: While pH and lipid effects on proteins are studied in isolation, this proposal uniquely posits that their interaction is the primary determinant of functionality, systematically testing this across a physiologically relevant pH and lipid landscape. It shifts the paradigm from a generic 'pH adjustment' strategy to a targeted 'compatibility pairing' approach for gel design.

proposals.title: Modulating Gelation and Emulsifying Properties of Soy Protein Isolate-Flaxseed Protein Blends via pH-Shifting: A Study on Network Synergy and Dysphagia Food Potential
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research shows plant protein blends can improve gel functionality, but synergistic mechanisms are poorly understood. pH-shifting is a known method to modify soy protein isolate (SPI) structure and gelation, yet its application to heterogeneous protein blends, particularly with underutilized proteins like flaxseed protein (FP), is unexplored. This gap limits the rational design of novel composite gels for specialized nutrition, such as texture-modified foods for dysphagia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pH-shifting treatment applied to SPI-FP blends will induce synergistic structural changes, leading to superior composite gel networks with enhanced rheological, water-holding, and emulsifying properties compared to gels from native or singly treated proteins.
proposals.method: SPI and FP will be blended at varying ratios (e.g., 100:0, 75:25, 50:50, 25:75, 0:100) and subjected to a pH-shifting cycle (adjust to pH 12, hold, then neutralize to pH 7). The structural changes will be characterized by surface hydrophobicity, solubility, and SDS-PAGE. Gelation properties will be assessed via dynamic rheology during heating/cooling and texture profile analysis. Emulsifying activity and stability of the treated blends will be measured. The performance of the resulting composite gels will be benchmarked against controls (native blends, singly treated proteins) and evaluated for potential as dysphagia food using instrumental texture and water-immobilizing tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a structure-function framework for engineering plant protein blends via pH-shifting, directly contributing to the field of protein composite gels. It will provide concrete formulations and processing conditions for creating gels with tailored textures, addressing a need in functional and elderly nutrition. The findings will expand the application of non-soy proteins like flaxseed in structured food systems.
proposals.novelty: While pH-shifting on single proteins is known, applying it to heterogeneous blends to deliberately create synergistic network interactions is a novel processing strategy. Combining SPI with FP, a protein studied for gels in Dr. Xiong's prior work, under this treatment to target dysphagia food applications is an unexplored approach.

proposals.title: pH-shifting-induced structural modifications of flaxseed protein: Impact on foaming capacity, gelation kinetics, and composite gel network formation with starch
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established pH-shifting as a promising, chemical-free method to modify protein functionality, primarily focusing on improving emulsifying activity and solubility. However, its systematic effects on other critical functional properties like foaming and gelation, which are essential for structuring foods like dysphagia diets and meat analogs, remain underexplored. This gap limits the targeted application of pH-shifted plant proteins, such as flaxseed protein, in complex food matrices where multiple functionalities are required.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pH-shifting (specifically through cycles of alkali and acid treatment) will induce distinct, quantifiable changes in the structural unfolding and re-aggregation of flaxseed protein, leading to predictable and tunable enhancements in its foaming properties and its ability to form synergistic composite gel networks with mung bean starch.
proposals.method: Flaxseed protein isolate will undergo pH-shifting treatments (e.g., to pH 12, hold, then adjust to pH 7). Treated proteins will be characterized for structural changes using surface hydrophobicity (ANS fluorescence), sulfhydryl group/disulfide bond content, and particle size distribution. Functionality will be assessed by measuring foaming capacity/stability, dynamic rheology of protein and protein-starch composite gels (monitoring G' and G'' during heating/cooling), and texture profile analysis of set gels. The composite gel system will mirror recent work on mung bean starch-flaxseed protein gels, with the novel variable being the pre-treatment of the protein via pH-shifting.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a clear structure-function relationship between pH-shifting parameters and the foaming/gelation properties of flaxseed protein. It will provide a practical framework for tailoring protein ingredients for specific food applications requiring aerated or gelled textures, particularly in the development of next-generation plant-based and dysphagia-friendly foods. The findings will expand the utility of pH-shifting beyond emulsification.
proposals.novelty: While pH-shifting's effect on emulsification is known, this study uniquely links specific structural metrics from the treatment to the concurrent modulation of foaming and gelation, and investigates its impact within a relevant composite starch-protein food matrix, which has not been systematically reported pre-2026.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Fracture of Protein Networks: A Mechanistic Model Linking Disulfide Cleavage to Emulsifying Properties
proposals.motivation: The emulsifying capacity of proteins is a critical functional property in food science, but its mechanistic basis remains largely qualitative. While disulfide bond cleavage is known to improve emulsification, the quantitative relationship between bond scission, protein network flexibility, and interfacial performance is poorly understood. A predictive, physics-based model is needed to rationally design protein ingredients with tailored functionalities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the emulsifying capacity of a globular protein can be quantitatively modeled as a function of disulfide bond cleavage, where the extent of bond scission directly governs the protein's network flexibility and surface activity, analogous to cross-link scission in polymer fracture mechanics.
proposals.method: We will use porcine myofibrillar protein (MP) as a model system, treating it with controlled concentrations of a reducing agent (e.g., dithiothreitol) to create a gradient of disulfide cleavage, quantified via Ellman's assay for free thiols. For each treatment, we will measure changes in protein flexibility (via dynamic rheology of protein gels) and surface activity (via interfacial rheology at an oil-water interface). These parameters will be correlated with emulsification performance metrics, including droplet size distribution and emulsion stability index, to develop a quantitative model linking bond scission to functional output.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for understanding and predicting protein functionality, moving beyond empirical correlations. It will establish a direct, causal link between molecular-level structural changes (disulfide cleavage) and macroscopic functional properties, offering a new tool for the rational design of protein-based ingredients and composite gels.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit adaptation of polymer network fracture mechanics concepts to protein science, treating disulfide bonds as mechanical cross-links, and in the quantitative integration of bond cleavage data with interfacial rheology to build a predictive model for emulsification.

proposals.title: Deconvoluting Covalent and Steric Roles of Disulfide Bonds in Soy Glycinin Gelation Using a Double-Mutant Cycle Approach
proposals.motivation: Disulfide bonds are critical for the structural integrity and functional properties, such as gelation and emulsification, of storage proteins like soy glycinin. However, traditional single-point cysteine mutations (e.g., Cys-to-Ser) conflate the loss of the covalent bond with the steric and chemical change of the amino acid substitution, leading to ambiguous interpretations of the disulfide's specific role in protein network formation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the specific covalent contribution of a disulfide bond to glycinin's thermal stability and gelation properties is distinct from and can be isolated from the general structural perturbation caused by mutating the cysteine residue.
proposals.method: We will employ a rigorous double-mutant cycle analysis targeting key disulfide bonds in glycinin. For each target cysteine pair, we will generate two mutant variants: Cys-to-Ala (removing the sulfur and reducing side-chain volume) and Cys-to-Ser (introducing a polar hydroxyl group). The biophysical and functional properties of wild-type and mutant proteins will be systematically compared using differential scanning calorimetry (for thermal stability), dynamic rheology (for gelation kinetics and modulus), and water-holding capacity measurements. The specific energetic contribution of the covalent bond will be calculated from the differential effects observed between the two mutant types.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, mechanistic understanding of how specific disulfide bonds contribute to the gel network properties of plant proteins. The findings will directly inform the rational design of glycinin variants or processing conditions to tailor gel texture and water immobilization, with applications in plant-based foods and dysphagia diets.
proposals.novelty: While mutant cycle analysis is established in enzymology, its application to deconvolute disulfide bond effects in plant protein gelation is novel. This approach moves beyond correlative studies to causally attribute functional changes to the covalent linkage versus local steric environment.

proposals.title: Nano-emulsion stabilization by pH-shifted myofibrillar and flaxseed proteins: Structure, stability, and potential for bioactive delivery in dysphagia foods
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research highlights the need for safe, food-grade nano-emulsions to deliver sensitive bioactives and nutrients, particularly for specialized populations like those with dysphagia. Protein-stabilized emulsions are promising, but conventional proteins often lack the interfacial stability for fine, long-lasting nano-droplets. Dr. Xiong's prior work on protein gel networks and composite systems provides a foundation to engineer proteins for superior emulsification.
proposals.hypothesis: Subjecting myofibrillar and plant proteins (e.g., flaxseed) to controlled pH-shift treatments will induce structural unfolding and partial aggregation, enhancing their surface activity and enabling the formation of ultra-stable, fine protein-stabilized nano-emulsions suitable for nutrient delivery.
proposals.method: Porcine myofibrillar protein and flaxseed protein isolate will undergo pH-shift treatment (e.g., cycling between pH 12 and 7). Treated proteins will be used to prepare oil-in-water nano-emulsions via high-pressure homogenization. Emulsion properties (droplet size, zeta-potential, microstructure) will be characterized using dynamic light scattering and confocal laser scanning microscopy. Stability will be assessed under environmental stresses (pH, ionic strength, heating), and rheological/textural properties will be evaluated, linking them to Dr. Xiong's expertise in gel networks for dysphagia foods.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a structure-function framework for using pH-shifted muscle and plant proteins as novel nano-emulsifiers. It will directly contribute to the design of next-generation composite food matrices for controlled delivery, bridging the gap between protein modification, emulsion science, and the development of texture-modified foods for clinical nutrition.
proposals.novelty: While pH-shifting is known to modify protein solubility, its targeted application to tailor the interfacial properties of muscle-derived proteins for nano-emulsion formation is unexplored. Combining this with plant proteins in a composite emulsifier system for dysphagia-focused delivery applications is a novel integration of Dr. Xiong's research themes.

proposals.title: Sequential pH-Triggered Emulsification and Gelation by a Single Protein for Structuring Emulsion Gels
proposals.motivation: Creating clean-label, multifunctional food ingredients is a major industry challenge. Current emulsion gels often require complex blends of emulsifiers and gelling agents, which complicates formulations and labeling. Exploiting the intrinsic, pH-dependent functionality of a single protein to perform sequential colloidal and textural roles would streamline production and enable precise control over fat and water immobilization in products like dysphagia foods or meat analogs.
proposals.hypothesis: A single protein source, such as flaxseed or mung bean protein, can be exploited to first stabilize a fine emulsion at its optimal emulsifying pH, and then, upon a controlled pH shift, form a continuous gel network without requiring additional gelling agents, creating a stable emulsion gel (emulgel).
proposals.method: We will select a pH-responsive plant protein (e.g., flaxseed protein) based on its known solubility profile. First, we will characterize its emulsifying activity and droplet size distribution at various pH values (e.g., pH 7-9) to identify the optimal emulsification window. A coarse emulsion will be prepared at this pH using high-shear mixing. Subsequently, the pH of the continuous phase will be precisely adjusted (e.g., to pH 4-5 near the protein's isoelectric point) using glucono-delta-lactone (GDL) for a slow acidification, triggering gelation. The sequential process will be monitored via rheology (time-sweeps), microscopy (CLSM), and texture analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for a simplified, single-ingredient strategy to create complex emulsion gel structures. It will provide fundamental insights into how a protein's dual functionalities can be decoupled and sequentially triggered by pH, contributing to the physics of soft food materials. The resulting knowledge can be applied to develop novel textured foods with minimal ingredients.
proposals.novelty: While pH-induced gelation of proteins is known, the strategic use of the same protein for two distinct, sequential structural roles—first as an emulsifier and then as a gelling matrix—within one continuous process is a novel application of smart material design principles to food protein engineering, moving beyond simple composite blending.

ideas.title: pH-Responsive Protein Architectures for Sequential Emulsification and Gelation
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from polymer chemistry's smart hydrogels. The analogous solution uses a single stimuli-responsive polymer that performs sequential functions—first as a colloidal stabilizer, then as a gel matrix former—upon a triggered change. Adapted to food science, the research would exploit a pH-shifted protein not just for two functions, but for two sequential, structurally dependent functions in one process stream. The protein is first used at a pH optimal for emulsification to create fine droplets. Then, within the same product batch, a precise, final pH adjustment is applied to trigger the gelation of the continuous phase, creating a combined emulsion gel (emulgel) where the protein acts as both the primary emulsifier and the sole gelling agent, simplifying formulations and creating novel textures.

ideas.title: Applying Polymer Network Fracture Mechanics to Model Protein Disulfide Cleavage and Emulsification
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from polymer fracture mechanics, where controlled cross-link scission is quantitatively linked to changes in material properties. The analogous field is Polymer Science, specifically the fracture mechanics of elastomers, where researchers measure how breaking cross-links alters network flexibility and macroscopic behavior. The adapted research idea treats disulfide bonds as cross-links in a protein's structural network. By quantitatively measuring the extent of disulfide bond cleavage (e.g., via free thiol assays) in parallel with interfacial rheology and emulsification performance tests (e.g., droplet size distribution, emulsion stability), the study will elucidate the causal chain: bond cleavage -> increased protein flexibility and surface activity -> improved emulsifying capacity. This provides a mechanistic, quantitative model for the observed phenomenon.

ideas.title: Double-mutant cycle analysis to isolate covalent from steric effects of disulfide bonds in glycinin
ideas.description: This redesigned approach employs a double-mutant cycle to deconvolute the specific role of the disulfide bond from the general structural impact of mutating cysteine. For each target disulfide, two mutants are created: Cys-to-Ser and Cys-to-Ala. The Cys-to-Ala mutant controls for the removal of the sulfur atom and associated side-chain volume, while the Cys-to-Ser mutant introduces a polar hydroxyl group. By comparing the biophysical properties (e.g., stability, solubility) of both mutants against the wild-type protein, the specific energetic and functional contribution of the covalent bond itself can be isolated, leading to a more rigorous and interpretable result.

ideas.title: In-situ correlative microscopy of shear-pH driven protein-lipid network assembly
ideas.description: This research direction integrates advanced electron microscopy (e.g., cryo-EM) with a novel processing method that applies controlled shear stress during pH-shifting of protein-lipid mixtures. By capturing high-resolution microstructural snapshots at precise timepoints during the coupled physicochemical-mechanical treatment, the work will map the evolution of the protein-lipid interface and gel network. This allows for the direct correlation of specific processing parameters (shear rate, pH trajectory) with the resulting nanoscale architecture, moving beyond endpoint characterization. The core novelty is the ability to deconstruct the dynamic assembly pathway, identifying how shear alters unfolding intermediates and directs lipid co-assembly to create networks with targeted mechanical and functional properties.

ideas.title: Mapping the pH-Lipid Interaction Landscape for Mild Functional Enhancement
ideas.description: This research direction systematically investigates whether the functional effects of mild, near-physiological pH shifts are universally applicable or are critically dependent on the specific type of dietary lipid (e.g., lard, tallow, olive oil, coconut oil) present. It posits that the synergy or antagonism between specific fatty acid profiles and minor pH changes is the key determinant for achieving functional improvement, rather than the pH shift alone. The goal is to map these interactions to identify tailored, mild processing conditions (specific pH-lipid pairs) that maximize functional benefits without requiring harsh, non-physiological environments. This moves the field from a one-size-fits-all approach to a precision modulation strategy based on biochemical compatibility.

It employs a high-throughput platform to screen formulations at low ionic strength against a multi-parameter profile including texture, stability, safety (via water activity prediction), and sensory proxies. The goal is to identify mechanistic trade-offs and engineer robust, multi-component strategies that deliver the full suite of required functionalities, enabling significant sodium reduction without compromising product quality or safety.

ideas.title: Adapting Polymer Folding Kinetics to Compare Acid vs. Alkaline pH-Shifting Efficacy
ideas.description: This research seed expands the comparative analysis of acid vs. alkaline pH-shifting by drawing an analogy from polymer and protein folding kinetics. In that analogous field, the efficacy of different solvent conditions in directing folding is solved by mapping the detailed kinetic pathways and intermediate states, not just comparing endpoints. Adapted to this context, the idea is to perform a time-resolved, mechanistic comparison of structural changes induced by acidic versus alkaline pH-shifts. Using techniques like stopped-flow fluorescence, time-resolved small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS), and molecular dynamics simulations, the study would delineate the distinct sequences of conformational changes—such as the formation, stability, and evolution of intermediate states like molten globules or early aggregates. This approach will determine which pH-shifting method is more efficacious for achieving specific structural targets (e.g., functional fibrils, stable monomers, or bioactive conformations) by revealing the underlying kinetic bottlenecks and transition states unique to each pathway.

ideas.title: Deconvolving emergent coupling effects of disulfide bonds within cooperative gel networks
ideas.description: This research moves beyond quantifying independent bond contributions to instead map the emergent, context-dependent role of disulfide bonds within a coupled interaction network. A systematic matrix of orthogonal chemical and physical perturbations is applied to model protein gels while simultaneously monitoring mechanical (rheology) and structural (spectroscopy) responses. The resulting high-dimensional dataset is used to parameterize a computational network model that identifies synergistic and antagonistic couplings between disulfide bonds, electrostatic, hydrophobic, and hydrogen bonding interactions, revealing how disulfide bonds modulate overall network stability and mechanics through these couplings.

ideas.title: Refold proteins at a pH other than 7.0 to create different functional states
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that refolding must aim for the native, pH-neutral state. Drawing an analogy from materials science, where pH controls the final functional state of conductive polymers like polyaniline, we adapt that solution to protein biochemistry. The analogous solution involved using pH as a dial to produce distinct oxidation/protonation states with different material properties. The adapted research idea is to systematically refold proteins across a broad pH gradient, treating pH as a variable to 'trap' the protein in different conformational ensembles. These non-native refolded states would then be screened for novel catalytic, regulatory, or ligand-binding functions that are not accessible under standard refolding conditions at pH 7.0, potentially creating a library of functional variants from a single sequence.

ideas.title: Dynamic Duration Mapping: A Multi-Factorial Framework for pH-Shifting Optimization
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a one-dimensional time scan by constructing a predictive model for pH-shifting outcomes. A high-throughput experimental design will systematically vary incubation time, protein source, initial purity, and final application test (e.g., emulsion stability, gel strength). The resulting multi-dimensional dataset will be used to train a machine learning model that identifies critical interactions and predicts the optimal treatment duration for any given starting material and functional goal. This approach transforms the optimization from a rigid prescription into an adaptable, knowledge-driven framework.

ideas.title: Mechanistically-Guided Isolation of the Causal Lipid Fraction Driving Metabolic Divergence
ideas.description: This study moves beyond a simple animal-vs-plant survey to test a specific mechanistic hypothesis for the observed lard-oil difference. Fats and oils are selected and grouped into defined tiers based on precise compositional profiles (e.g., SFA:MUFA:PUFA ratios, phytosterol content) predicted by the hypothesis. The experimental diet system uses a factorial design where these lipid profiles are exchanged into otherwise nutritionally identical, fully defined diets. This approach isolates the causal biochemical component of the fat, determining whether the effect is driven by fatty acid chains, minor bioactive lipids, or an interaction, thereby generating a generalizable rule rather than a list of individual fat effects.

ideas.title: Challenging the Dominance of 11S Globulin Reactivity via Targeted pH-Induced Conformational Gating
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that 11S globulins are always more responsive than 7S globulins under pH-shifting conditions by employing an analogical framework from enzyme kinetics. In that field, the performance hierarchy of enzyme isoforms can be inverted through targeted allosteric regulation, where specific environmental triggers selectively activate one isoform while deactivating another. The analogous solution involves precise tuning of conditions to achieve conformational gating. Adapted to this protein chemistry context, the research will systematically test if modified pH-shifting conditions—characterized by highly specific target pH values, rates of pH transition, and dwell times—can act as a similar conformational gate. The goal is to discover a protocol that selectively stabilizes a high-reactivity state in 7S globulins while simultaneously driving 11S globulins into a less reactive or aggregated form, thereby making 7S more responsive than 11S and overturning the standard assumption.

ideas.title: Temporally resolved shear perturbation of pH-jump protein unfolding pathways
ideas.description: This redesigned approach uses a sequential, time-resolved protocol to dissect the interplay between chemical and mechanical unfolding. A rapid pH-jump is first applied to initiate protein unfolding under quiescent conditions, establishing a baseline pathway. After a precisely controlled delay, a defined shear field is introduced to perturb the unfolding ensemble at a specific stage of its evolution. This method enables the isolation of shear's effect on early unfolding intermediates versus later, more denatured states, providing clear mechanistic insight into how mechanical forces alter folding landscapes and aggregation kinetics initiated by pH.

ideas.title: Use microfluidic pH gradients with integrated optical sensing for high-throughput protein stability profiling
ideas.description: This idea expands the original microfluidics-based pH gradient concept by drawing an analogy from chemical ecology methods for studying root growth. The analogous solution uses agar plates with pH indicator dyes to create visible, stable gradients for optical analysis of biological responses. This is adapted by integrating pH-sensitive fluorescent reporter dyes into the microfluidic carrier buffer, creating a co-flowing, optically active pH gradient. Proteins are exposed to this continuous gradient, and their unfolding is monitored via fluorescence (e.g., FRET, quenching, or intrinsic tryptophan signals) using microscopy. The simultaneous, spatially-correlated optical readout of both local pH and protein conformational state allows for the automated, high-throughput identification of critical unfolding thresholds with high precision.

ideas.title: pH-Driven Destabilization Refolding for Functional Metamorphosis
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to deliberately refold proteins at non-physiological pH extremes not to recover native structure, but to trap them in metastable, partially unfolded states with significant secondary structure loss. By challenging the assumption that this loss is detrimental, the approach aims to discover if such pH-induced 'destabilized' folds can gain novel catalytic activities, substrate specificities, or material properties absent in the native state. The synergy lies in using pH as a precise, tunable experimental variable to map a landscape of functional outcomes against defined degrees of structural perturbation, moving beyond anecdotal observation to a predictive framework. This could enable the engineering of protein 'metamorphs' whose function is switched by the pH history of their refolding, rather than their current environment.

ideas.title: Achieve Functional Shifts via Localized, Mild pH Modulation Using Buffered Nanoreactors
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that achieving a desired functional improvement (e.g., enzyme activation, protein folding, material synthesis) requires shifting the entire experimental or physiological environment to extreme pH values. By drawing an analogy from heterogeneous catalysis, where localized microenvironments on catalysts enable reactions under milder bulk conditions, we adapt the solution to biochemistry and biomaterials. The adapted research idea is to engineer nanoscale compartments or scaffolds that function as 'pH nanoreactors.' These would incorporate internal buffering agents or proton pumps to create and sustain a precise, mild pH shift (e.g., slightly acidic or basic) locally around the target molecules or catalysts, while the bulk solution remains near physiological pH. This approach could enable sensitive biocatalysis, controlled protein assembly, or therapeutic activation with reduced stress and damage to the broader system.

ideas.title: Assume fat type effect is not universal: Adapting the 'Broad-Spectrum vs. Narrow-Spectrum' framework from pharmacology
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the observed difference between lard and a specific plant oil is a universal property of 'animal fat vs. plant oil.' Drawing an analogy from pharmacology's method of distinguishing broad-spectrum from narrow-spectrum drugs, the research adapts the solution of panel testing. The analogous solution involves moving from a single case study to systematic screening against a diverse target panel to define the scope of an effect. Adapted here, this means constructing a standardized experimental panel of diverse fats and oils—varying in source (ruminant, poultry, marine, fruit, seed) and chemical composition. By testing the original outcome across this panel under controlled conditions, the research will map the effect's range, determining if it is 'broad-spectrum' (applies to most animal vs. plant fats) or 'narrow-spectrum' (driven by specific subtypes like saturated ruminant fats vs. high-PUFA seed oils), thereby identifying the precise structural or compositional constraints of the initial observation.

ideas.title: Synergistic Multi-Stimuli Control for Physiological-PH-Driven Functional Enhancement
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the inherent weakness of mild pH shifts by coupling them with complementary, non-destructive stimuli to create a potent, targeted control system. By operating within a narrow, near-physiological pH window, it maintains biocompatibility and system integrity. The synergistic combination of stimuli is designed to multiplicatively lower the energetic barriers for functional changes, achieving robust outcomes comparable to extreme pH treatments. This enables precise modulation of protein function, material properties, or cellular processes in complex biological environments.

ideas.title: Correlative Cryo-Electron Microscopy and X-ray Tomography for 3D Gel Network Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts a correlative imaging solution from materials science and geophysics to the analysis of gel microstructures. The analogous problem involves characterizing the 3D microstructure of complex, multi-phase materials like rocks or synthetic foams, which is solved by combining high-resolution 2D imaging (e.g., SEM for nanoscale detail) with lower-resolution 3D volumetric imaging (e.g., X-ray micro-CT for spatial context) and computationally fusing the datasets. Adapted to the research context, we propose using correlative cryo-electron microscopy and cryo-soft X-ray tomography. Cryo-EM will provide the fine, 2D detail of the protein-lipid interface and network morphology, while cryo-SXT will map the 3D architecture and connectivity of the same gel sample volume. By developing image registration and data fusion techniques, this approach will generate a comprehensive, multiscale 3D model of the gel microstructure, revealing how nanoscale interfacial properties dictate the mesoscale network organization and mechanical function.

ideas.title: Engineering Functional Allostery and Adaptability via Controlled Local Secondary Structure Destabilization
ideas.description: This research tests the hypothesis that the deliberate, localized destabilization of secondary structure elements can be a design principle for creating novel protein functions, specifically enhanced allosteric control or substrate adaptability. Using computational protein design, we will engineer variants of model enzymes and binding proteins where key alpha-helices or beta-strands are systematically weakened or replaced with more flexible linkers. The functional impact of these designed 'structured disorder' regions will be rigorously quantified through high-throughput activity assays, binding measurements, and structural analysis (e.g., HDX-MS, NMR) to establish causal links between defined structural loss and beneficial functional outcomes, moving beyond correlation to mechanism.

ideas.title: Closed-loop microfluidic pH gradient platform with real-time simulation feedback for predictive protein stability mapping
ideas.description: This research integrates a high-precision microfluidic pH gradient generator with a real-time molecular dynamics simulation engine. As a protein traverses the experimentally imposed pH gradient, its predicted structural response from the simulation is used to dynamically adjust the gradient profile, focusing resolution on computationally predicted unfolding transition points. This closed-loop system allows for the direct experimental validation of simulation predictions at the exact predicted conditions, creating a powerful tool for refining force fields and understanding pH-driven denaturation. The synergy enables the efficient mapping of protein stability landscapes with unprecedented predictive accuracy and experimental efficiency.

ideas.title: Employ computational protein folding simulations with continuum solvent models from civil engineering
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from civil engineering, specifically the continuum modeling of pore fluids in soil mechanics, to the problem of simulating pH-dependent protein folding. In geotechnics, engineers model soil deformation under changing water conditions using continuum hydro-mechanical models instead of explicit fluid molecules. By applying this analogous concept, we propose to enhance computational protein folding simulations by employing advanced continuum solvent models (e.g., pH-responsive Poisson-Boltzmann) within the molecular dynamics framework. This adaptation allows for the efficient prediction of protein structural changes in response to pH shifts by modeling the solvent's electrostatic properties as a responsive continuum, dramatically reducing computational cost while capturing the essential physicochemical effects of the pH-changing process.

ideas.title: Kinetic Pathway Steering for Functional Recovery of pH-Denatured Proteins
ideas.description: This project develops a dynamic, pathway-aware platform to recover functional protein structures from pH-induced denatured states, which often contain irreversible damage. Instead of assuming simple reversibility, the system uses real-time structural feedback from multiple biophysical probes to map the energy landscape and identify off-pathway traps. It then employs a closed-loop control system to combinatorially apply kinetic steering factors—such as chaperones, osmolytes, and transient ligands—that actively guide the polypeptide chain around kinetic barriers and toward the native functional conformation, even when the exact initial native state is no longer thermodynamically accessible.

ideas.title: Protein Renaturation via Signal Reconstruction from Corrupted Data
ideas.description: This research adapts the signal reconstruction framework from telecommunications to solve the protein renaturation inverse problem. In signal processing, a corrupted transmission is recovered by comparing it to an encoding model and applying iterative corrections. Analogously, a pH-denatured protein is treated as a structurally 'corrupted' version of its native state. The solution involves developing a feedback-controlled biophysical process where the protein's experimentally observed denatured state (via spectroscopic probes) is continuously compared against a computational model of its native energy landscape. A machine learning controller then makes a series of small, incremental adjustments to the buffer pH, ionic strength, and chaperone concentrations, applying a form of 'structural error correction' to steer the protein's folding pathway back to its original native conformation, effectively renaturing it by minimizing the thermodynamic divergence from its corrupted state.

ideas.title: High-throughput screening of pH-shift conditions using a Design of Experiments (DoE) approach from materials science
ideas.description: This idea adapts the statistical Design of Experiments (DoE) methodology, widely used in fields like materials science for high-throughput discovery of alloys, to the high-throughput screening of biochemical pH-shift conditions. The analogous problem in materials science involves exploring a vast, multi-dimensional composition space with robotic synthesizers; it is solved by using DoE to select a strategic, non-exhaustive set of samples that maximizes information about interactions and optimal regions. Adapted here, a robotic liquid handling platform would be programmed to prepare and test a DoE-defined matrix of pH, incubation time, and salt concentration combinations for a process like protein refolding or precipitation. The resulting data is analyzed to build a response surface model, efficiently identifying optimal condition ranges and critical parameter interactions that would be missed by a conventional one-factor-at-a-time or dense grid search.

ideas.title: Embracing Structural Disruption: A Biomimetic Approach to Functional Gain
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that structural loss is always detrimental by drawing an analogy from developmental biology, specifically limb regeneration. In that field, the solution to rebuilding complex structures involves a necessary phase of structural breakdown and de-differentiation (forming a blastema), which enables subsequent re-specification into new, functional tissues. Adapted to protein science, this suggests a research program to explore if a designed, greater loss of secondary structure could be a beneficial, intermediate step. The adapted idea is to engineer proteins with triggers (e.g., pH, ligand, light) that induce a controlled de-structuring phase, moving the chain away from its native fold. The hypothesis is that this transiently disordered or molten globule-like state could then be guided (via evolved sequence cues or external scaffolds) to collapse into a novel, stable, and functional tertiary structure, thereby turning structural loss into a gateway for functional innovation.

ideas.title: Apply Pharmacokinetic Modeling to Determine Optimal pH-Shift Duration
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to determining optimal treatment duration from pharmacology. In drug development, pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) modeling is used to find the dosing schedule that maximizes therapeutic benefit within a safe window. The analogous solution involves measuring the time-course of drug effect to model the exposure-response relationship. Adapted to the pH-shifting context, the research would systematically vary incubation time (the 'exposure') and quantitatively measure multiple functional responses (the 'dynamics') at frequent intervals. The resulting dataset would be used to construct time-response curves for each benefit metric (e.g., protein functionality). Mathematical analysis of these curves, akin to PK/PD modeling, would then identify the precise optimal treatment window—the incubation duration that peaks desired properties before the onset of degradation or diminishing returns—moving beyond a single fixed time point like 4 hours to a dynamically defined optimum.
